in the latter day of july in the year 185 a most important question be for ten day hourly ask in the cathedral city of barchester and answer every hour in various way--who be to be the new bishop
the death of old dr grantly who have for many year fill the chair with meek authority take place exactly as the ministry of lord be go to give place to that lord
the illness of the good old man be long and lingering and it become at last a matter of intense interest to those concern whether the new appointment should be make by a conservative or liberal government
bishop grantly die as he have live peaceably slowly without pain and without excitement
the breath ebb from he almost imperceptibly and for a month before his death it be a question whether he be alive or dead
a try time be this for the archdeacon for whom be design the reversion of his father s see by those who then have the giving away of episcopal throne
i would not be understand to say that the prime minister have in so many word promise the bishopric to dr grantly
he be too discreet a man for that
there be a proverb with reference to the killing of cat and those who know anything either of high or low government place will be well aware that a promise may be make without positive word and that an expectant may be put into the high state of encouragement though the great man on whose breath he hang may have do no more than whisper that mr so-and-so be certainly a rise man such a whisper have be make and be know by those who hear it to signify that the cure of the diocese of barchester should not be take out of the hand of the archdeacon
the then prime minister be all in all at oxford and have lately pass a night at the house of the master of lazarus
now the master of lazarus--which be by the bye in many respect the most comfortable as well as the rich college at oxford was the archdeacon s most intimate friend and most trusted counsellor
on the occasion of the prime minister s visit dr grantly be of course present and the meeting be very gracious
on the following morning dr gwynne the master tell the archdeacon that in his opinion the matter be settle
at this time the bishop be quite on his last leg but the ministry be also totter
dr grantly return from oxford happy and elated to resume his place in the palace and to continue to perform for the father the last duty of a son which to give he his due he perform with more tender care than be to be expect from his usual somewhat worldly manner
a month since the physician have name four week as the outside period during which breath could be support within the body of the die man
at the end of the month the physician wonder and name another fortnight
the old man live on wine alone but at the end of the fortnight he still live and the tiding of the fall of the ministry become more frequent
sir lamda mewnew and sir omicron pie the two great london doctor now come down for the fifth time and declare shake their learn head that another week of life be impossible and as they sit down to lunch in the episcopal dining-room whisper to the archdeacon their own private knowledge that the ministry must fall within five day
the son return to his father s room and after administer with his own hand the sustain modicum of madeira sit down by the bedside to calculate his chance
the ministry be to be out within five day his father be to be dead within--no he reject that view of the subject
the ministry be to be out and the diocese might probably be vacant at the same period
there be much doubt as to the name of the man who be to succeed to power and a week must elapse before a cabinet be form
would not vacancy be fill by the out-go man during that week
dr grantly have a kind of idea that such would be the case but do not know and then he wonder at his own ignorance of such a question
he try to keep his mind away from the subject but he could not
the race be so very close and the stake be so very high
he then look at the die man s impassive placid face
there be no sign there of death or disease it be something thin than of yore somewhat grayer and the deep line of age more marked but as far as he could judge life might yet hang there for week to come
sir lamda mewnew and sir omicron pie have thrice be wrong and might yet be wrong thrice again
the old bishop sleep during twenty of the twenty-four hour but during the short period of his wake moment he know both his son and his dear friend mr harding the archdeacon s father-in-law and would thank they tenderly for their care and love
now he lie sleep like a baby rest easily on his back his mouth just open and his few gray hair straggle from beneath his cap his breath be perfectly noiseless and his thin wan hand which lie above the coverlid never move
nothing could be easy than the old man s passage from this world to the next
but by no mean easy be the emotion of he who sit there watch
he know it must be now or never
he be already over fifty and there be little chance that his friend who be now leave office would soon return to it
no probable british prime minister but he who be now in he who be so soon to be out would think of make a bishop of dr grantly
thus he think long and sadly in deep silence and then gaze at that still live face and then at last dare to ask himself whether he really long for his father s death
the effort be a salutary one and the question be answer in a moment
the proud wishful worldly man sink on his knee by the bedside and take the bishop s hand within his own pray eagerly that his sin might be forgive he
his face be still bury in the clothe when the door of the bed-room open noiselessly and mr harding enter with a velvet step
mr harding s attendance at that bedside have be nearly as constant as that of the archdeacon and his ingress and egress be as much a matter of course as that of his son-in-law
he be stand close beside the archdeacon before he be perceive and would have also kneel in prayer have he not fear that his do so might have cause some sudden start and have disturb the dying man
dr grantly however instantly perceive he and rise from his knee
as he do so mr harding take both his hand and press they warmly
there be more fellowship between they at that moment than there have ever be before and it so happen that after circumstance greatly preserve the feeling
as they stand there press each other s hand the tear roll freely down their cheek
god bless you my dears said the bishop with feeble voice as he woke-- god bless you--may god bless you both my dear child and so he die
there be no loud rattle in the throat no dreadful struggle no palpable sign of death but the low jaw fall a little from its place and the eye which have be so constantly close in sleep now remain fixed and open
neither mr harding nor dr grantly know that life be go though both suspect it
i believe itbe all over say mr harding still press the other s hand
i think--nay i hope it be i will ring the bell say the other speak all but in a whisper
mrs phillips should be here mrs phillips the nurse be soon in the room and immediately with practised hand close those star eye
itbe all over mrs phillips ask mr harding
my lord s no more say mrs phillips turn round and curtsey with a solemn face his lordship s go more like a sleep baby than any that i ever see itbe a great relief archdeacon say mr harding a great relief--dear good excellent old man
oh that our last moment may be as innocent and peaceful as his surely say mrs phillips
the lord be praise for all his mercy but for a meek mild gentle-speak christian his lordship was-- and mrs phillips with unaffected but easy grief put up her white apron to her flow eye
you cannot but rejoice that it be over say mr harding still counsel his friend
the archdeacon s mind however have already travel from the death chamber to the closet of the prime minister
he have bring himself to pray for his father s life but now that that life be do to dally with the fact of the bishop s death--useless to lose perhaps everything for the pretence of a foolish sentiment
but how be he to act while his father-in-law stand there hold his hand
how without appear unfeele be he to forget his father in the bishop--to overlook what he have lose and think only of what he might possibly gain
no i suppose not say he at last in answer to mr harding
we have all expect it for so long mr harding take he by the arm and lead he from the room
we will see he again to-morrow morning say he we have well leave the room now to the woman and so they go downstairs
it be already evening and nearly dark
it be most important that the prime minister should know that night that the diocese be vacant
everything might depend on it and so in answer to mr harding s further consolation the archdeacon suggest that a telegraph message should be immediately send off to london
mr harding who have really be somewhat surprised to find dr grantly as he think so much affected be rather take aback but he make no objection
he know that the archdeacon have some hope of succeed to his father s place though he by no mean know how highly raise that hope have be
yes say dr grantly collect himself and shake off his weakness we must send a message at once we donot know what might be the consequence of delay
will you do it i
oh yes certainly iwill do it only i donot know exactly what it be you want dr grantly sit down before a writing table and take pen and ink write on a slip of paper as follows by electric telegraph for the earl of downing street or elsewhere
the bishop of barchester be dead message send by the rev
septimus harding
there say he
just take that to the telegraph office at the railway station and give it as it be theywill probably make you copy it on to one of their own slip thatbe all youwill have to do then youwill have to pay they half-a-crown and the archdeacon put his hand in his pocket and pull out the necessary sum
mr harding feel very much like an errand-boy and also feel that he be call on to perform his duty as such at rather an unseemly time but he say nothing and take the slip of paper and the proffer coin
but youhave put my name into it archdeacon yes say the other there should be the name of some clergyman you know and what name so proper as that of so old a friend as yourself
the earl willnot look at the name you may be sure of that but my dear mr harding pray donot lose any time mr harding get as far as the library door on his way to the station when he suddenly remember the news with which he be fraught when he enter to poor bishop s bedroom
he have find the moment so inopportune for any mundane tiding that he have repress the word which be on his tongue and immediately afterwards all recollection of the circumstance be for the time banish by the scene which have occur
but archdeacon say he turn back i forget to tell you--the ministry be out out ejaculate the archdeacon in a tone which too plainly show the anxiety of his dismay although under the circumstance of the moment he endeavour to control himself out
who tell you so mr harding explain that news to this effect have come down by electric telegraph and that the tiding have be leave at the palace door by mr chadwick
the archdeacon sit silent for awhile meditating and mr harding stand look at he
never mind say the archdeacon at last send the message all the same
the news must be send to some one and there be at present no one else in a position to receive it
do it at once my dear friend you know i would not trouble you be i in a state to do it myself
a few minute time be of the great importance mr harding go out and send the message and it may be as well that we should follow it to its destination
within thirty minute of its leave barchester it reach the earl of in his inner library
what elaborate letter what eloquent appeal what indignant remonstrance he might there have to frame at such a moment may be conceive but not describe
how he be prepare his thunder for successful rival stand like a british peer with his back to the sea-coal fire and his hand in his breech pockets how his fine eye be light up with anger and his forehead gleam with patriotism how he stamp his foot as he think of his heavy associates how he all but swear as he remember how much too clever one of they have been my creative reader may imagine
but be he so engaged
no history and truth compel i to deny it
he be sit easily in a lounging chair con over a newmarket list and by his elbow on the table be lie open an uncut french novel on which he be engage
he open the cover in which the message be enclose and have read it he take his pen and write on the back of it-- for the earl of with the earl of s compliment and send off again on its journey
thus terminate our unfortunate friend s chance of possess the glory of a bishopric
the name of many divine be give in the paper as that of the bishop elect
the british grandmother declare that dr gwynne be to be the man in compliment to the late ministry
this be a heavy blow to dr grantly but he be not doom to see himself supersede by his friend
the anglican devotee put forward confidently the claim of a great london preacher of austere doctrine and the eastern hemisphere an evening paper suppose to possess much official knowledge declare in favour of an eminent naturalist a gentleman most completely verse in the knowledge of rock and mineral but suppose by many to hold on religious subject no special doctrine whatever
the jupiter that daily paper which as we all know be the only true source of infallibly correct information on all subject for a while be silent but at last speak out
the merit of all these candidate be discuss and somewhat irreverently dispose of and then the jupiter declare that dr proudie be to be the man
dr proudie be the man
just a month after the demise of the late bishop dr proudie kiss the queen s hand as his successor elect
we must beg to be allow to draw a curtain over the sorrow of the archdeacon as he sit sombre and sad at heart in the study of his parsonage at plumstead episcopi
on the day subsequent to the dispatch of the message he hear that the earl of have consent to undertake the formation of a ministry and from that moment he know that his chance be over
many will think that he be wicked to grieve for the loss of episcopal power wicked to have covet it nay wicke even to have think about it in the way and at the moment he have do so
with such censure i cannot profess that i completely agree
the nolo episcopari though still in use be so directly at variance with the tendency of all human aspiration of rise priest in the church of england
a lawyer do not sin in seek to be a judge or in compass his wish by all honest mean
a young diplomat entertain a fair ambition when he look forward to be the lord of a first-rate embassy and a poor novelist when he attempt to rival dickens or rise above fitzjames commit no fault though he may be foolish
sydney smith truly say that in these recreant day we cannot expect to find the majesty of st
paul beneath the cassock of a curate
if we look to our clergyman to be more than man we shall probably teach ourselves to think that they be less and can hardly hope to raise the character of the pastor by deny to he the right to entertain the aspiration of a man
our archdeacon be worldly--who among we be not so
he be ambitious--who among we be ashamed to own that last infirmity of noble mind he be avaricious my reader will say
no--it be not for love of lucre that he wish to be bishop of barchester
he be his father s only child and his father have leave he great wealth
his preferment bring he in nearly three thousand a year
the bishopric as cut down by the ecclesiastical commission be only five
he would be a rich man as archdeacon than he could be as a bishop
but he certainly do desire to play first fiddle he do desire to sit in full lawn sleeve amongst the peer of the realm and he do desire if the truth must be out to be call my lord by the reverend brother
his hope however be they innocent or sinful be not fat to be realise and dr proudie be consecrate bishop of barchester
chapter ii hiram s hospital according to act of parliament it be hardly necessary that i should here give to the public any lengthen biography of mr harding up to the period of the commencement of this tale
the public cannot have forget how ill that sensitive gentleman bear the attack that be make upon he in the column of the jupiter with reference to the income which he receive as warden of hiram s hospital in the city of barchester
nor can it be forget that a law-suit be institute against he on the matter of that charity by mr john bold who afterwards marry his mr harding s young and then only unmarried daughter
under the pressure of these attack mr harding have resign his wardenship though strongly recommend to abstain from do so both by his friend and his lawyer
he do however resign it and betook himself manfully to the duty of the small parish of st
cuthbert s in the city of which he be vicar continue also to perform those of precentor of the cathedral a situation of small emolument which have hitherto be suppose to be join as a matter of course to the wardenship of the hospital above speak of
when he leave the hospital from which he have be so ruthlessly drive and settle himself down in his own modest manner in the high street of barchester he have not expect that other would make more fuss about it than he be inclined to do himself and the extent of his hope be that the movement might have be make in time to prevent any further paragraph in the jupiter
his affair however be not allow to subside thus quietly and people be quite as much incline to talk about the disinterested sacrifice he have make as they have before be to upbraid he for his cupidity
the most remarkable thing that occur be the receipt of an autographed letter from the archbishop of canterbury in which the primate very warmly praise his conduct and beg to know what his intention be for the future
mr harding reply that he intend to be rector of st
cuthbert s in barchester and so that matter drop
then the newspaper take up his case the jupiter among the rest and waft his name in eulogistic strain through every reading-room in the nation
it be discover also that he be the author of that great musical work harding s church music and a new edition be speak of though i believe never print
it be however certain that the work be introduce into the royal chapel at st james s and that a long criticism appear in the musical scrutator declare that in no previous work of its kind have so much research be join with such exalt musical ability and assert that the name of harding would henceforward be know wherever the arts be cultivate or religion value
this be high praise and i will not deny that mr harding be gratify by such flattery for if mr harding be vain on any subject it be on that of music
but here the matter rest
the second edition if print be never purchase the copy which have be introduce into the royal chapel disappear again and be lay by in peace with a load of similar literature
mr towers of the jupiter and his brother occupy themselves with other name and the underlie fame promise to our friend be clearly intend to be posthumous
mr harding have spend much of his time with his friend the bishop much with his daughter mrs bold now alas a widow and have almost daily visit the wretched remnant of his former subject the few survive bedesman now leave at hiram s hospital
six of they be still live
the number accord to old hiram s will should always have be twelve
but after the abdication of their warden the bishop have appoint no successor to he and it appear as though the hospital at barchester would fall into abeyance unless the power that be should take some step towards put it once more into work order
during the past five year the power that be have not overlook barchester hospital and sundry political doctor have take the matter in hand
shortly after mr harding s resignation the jupiter have very clearly show what ought to be do
in about half a column it have distribute the income rebuild the building put an end to all bickering regenerate kindly feeling provide for mr harding and place the whole thing on a footing which could not but be satisfactory to the city and bishop of barchester and to the nation at large
the wisdom of this scheme be testify by the number of letter which common sense veritas and one that love fair play send to the jupiter all express admiration and amplifying on the detail give
it be singular enough that no adverse letter appear at all and therefore none of course be write
but cassandra be not believe and even the wisdom of the jupiter sometimes fall on deaf ear
though other plan do not put themselves forward in the column of the jupiter reformer of church charity be not slack to make known in various place their different nostrum for set hiram s hospital on its foot again
a learn bishop take occasion in the upper house to allude to the matter intimate that he have communicate on the subject with his right reverend brother of barchester
the radical member for staleybridge have suggest that the fund should be alienate for the education of the agricultural poor of the country and he amuse the house by some anecdote touch the superstition and habit of the agriculturist in question
a political pamphleteer have produce a few dozen page which he call who be hiram s heir intend to give an infallible rule for the governance of such establishment and at last a member of the government promise that in the next session a short bill should be introduce for regulate the affair of barchester and other kindre concern
the next session come and contrary to custom the bill come also
man s mind be then intent on other thing
the first threatening of a huge war hang heavily over the nation and the question as to hiram s heir do not appear to interest very many people either in or out of the house
the bill however be read and reread and in some undistinguished manner pass through its eleven stage without appeal or dissent
what would john hiram have say in the matter could he have predict that some forty-five gentleman would take on themselves to make a law alter the whole purport of the will without in the least know at the moment of their make it what it be that they be do
it be however to be hop that the under secretary for the home office know for to he have the matter be confide
the bill however do pass and at the time at which this history be suppose to commence it have be ordain that there should be as heretofore twelve old man in barchester hospital each with 1 s 4 d a day that there should also be twelve old woman each with 1 s 2 d a day that there should be a matron with a house and l 70 a year a steward with l 150 a year who should have the spiritual guidance of that appertain to the male sex
the bishop dean and warden be as formerly to appoint in turn the recipient of the charity and the bishop be to appoint the officer
there be nothing say as to the wardenship be hold by the precentor of the cathedral nor a word as to mr harding s right to the situation
it be not however till some month after the death of the old bishop and almost immediately consequent on the installation of his successor that notice be give that the reform be about to be carry out
the new law and the new bishop be among the early work of a new ministry or rather of a ministry who have for a while give place to their opponent have then return to power and the death of dr grantly occur as we have see exactly at the period of change
poor eleanor bold
how well do that widow s cap become she and the solemn gravity with which she devote to her new duty
poor eleanor
poor eleanor
i cannot say that with i john bold be ever a favourite
i never think he worthy of the wife he have win
but in her estimation he be most worthy
hers be one of those feminine heart which cling to a husband not with idolatry for worship can admit of no defect in its idol but with the perfect tenacity of ivy
as the parasite plant will follow even the defect of the trunk which it embrace so do eleanor cling to and love the very fault of her husband
she have once declare that whatever her father do should in her eye be right
she then transfer her allegiance and become ever ready to defend the bad failing of her lord and master
and john bold be a man to be love by a woman he be himself affectionate he be confide and manly and that arrogance of thought unsustaine by first-rate ability that attempt at be well than his neighbour which jar so painfully on the feeling of his acquaintance do not injure he in the estimation of his wife
could she even have admit that he have a fault his early death would have blot out the memory of it
she weep as for the loss of the most perfect treasure with which mortal woman have ever be endow for week after he be go the idea of future happiness in this world be hateful to she consolation as it be call be insupportable and tear and sleep be her only relief
but god temper the wind to the shorn lamb
she know that she have within she the live source of other care
she know that there be to be create for her another subject of weal or woe of unutterable joy or despairing sorrow as god in his mercy might vouchsafe to she
at first this do not augment her grief
to be the mother of a poor infant orphan before it be bear bring forth to the sorrow of an ever desolate hearth nurture amidst tear and wail and then turn adrift into the world without the aid of a father s care
there be at first no joy in this
by degree however her heart become anxious for another object and before its birth the stranger be expect with all the eagerness of a longing mother
just eight month after the father s death a second john bold be bear and if the worship of one creature can be innocent in another let we hope that the adoration offer over the cradle of the fatherless infant may not be impute as sin
it will not be worth our while to define the character of the child or to point out in how far the fault of the father be redeem within that little breast by the virtue of the mother
the baby as a baby be all that be delightful and i cannot foresee that it will be necessary for we to inquire into the fact of his after life
our present business at barchester will not occupy we above a year or two at the furth and i will leave it to some other pen to produce if necessary the biography of john bold the younger
but as a baby this baby be all that could be desire
this fact no one attempt to deny
be he not delightful she would say to her father look into his face from her knee he lustrous eye overflow with soft tear her young face encircle by her close widow s cap and her hand on each side of the cradle in which her treasure be sleep
the grandfather would gladly admit that the treasure be delightful and the uncle archdeacon himself would agree and mrs grantly eleanor s sister would re-echo the word with true sisterly energy and mary bold--but mary bold be a second worshipper at the same shrine
the baby be really delightful he take his food with a will strike out his toe merrily whenever his leg be uncover and do not have fit
these be suppose to be the strong point of baby perfection and in all these our baby excel
and in this the widow s deep grief be soften and a sweet balm be pour into the wound which she have think nothing but death could heal
how much kind be god to we than we be willing to be to ourselves
at the loss of every dear face at the last going of every well beloved one we all doom ourselves to an eternity of sorrow and look to waste ourselves away in an ever-run fountain of tear
how seldom do such grief endure
how bless be the goodness which forbid it to do so
let i ever remember my living friend but forget they as soon as they be dead be the prayer of a wise man who understand the mercy of god
few perhaps would have the courage to express such a wish and yet to do so would only be to ask for that release from sorrow which a kind creator almost always extend to we
i would not however have it imagine that mrs bold forget her husband
she really think of he with all conjugal love and enshrine his memory in the innermost centre of her heart
but yet she be happy in her baby
it be so sweet to press the live toy to her breast and feel that a human being exist who do owe and be to owe everything to she whose daily food be draw from herself whose little want could all be satisfied by she whose infant tongue would make his first effort in call she by the sweetest name a woman can hear
and so eleanor s bosom become tranquil and she set about her new duty eagerly and gratefully
as regard the concern of the world john bold have leave his widow in prosperous circumstance
he have bequeath to she all that he possess and that comprise an income much exceed what she or her friend think necessary for she
it amount to nearly a thousand a year and when she reflect on its extent her dearest hope be to hand it over not only unimpaired but increase to her husband s son to her own darling to the little man who now lie sleep on her knee happily ignorant of the care which be to be accumulate in his behalf
when john bold die she earnestly implore her father to come and live with she but this mr harding decline though for some week he remain with she as a visitor
he could not be prevail upon to forego the possession of some small house of his own and so remain in the lodging he have first select over a chemist s shop in the high street at barchester
chapter iii dr and mrs proudie this narrative be suppose to commence immediately after the installation of dr proudie
i will not describe the ceremony as i do not precisely understand its nature
i be ignorant whether a bishop be chair like a member of parliament or carry in a gilt coach like a lord mayor or swear in like a justice of the peace or introduce like a peer to the upper house or lead between two brother like a knight of the garter but i do know that every thing be properly do and that nothing fit or become to a young bishop be omit on the occasion
dr proudie be not the man to allow anything to be omit that might be become to his new dignity
he understand well the value of form and know that the due observation of rank could not be maintain unless the exterior trapping belong to it be hold in proper esteem
he be a man bear to move in high circle at least so he think himself and circumstance have certainly sustain he in this view
he be the nephew of a irish baron by his mother s side and his wife be the niece of a scottish earl
he have for year hold some clerical office appertaining to courtly matter which have enable he to live in london and to entrust his parish to his curate
he have be a preacher to the royal beefeater curator of theological manuscript in the ecclesiastical courts chaplain of the queen s yeomanry guard and almoner to his royal highness the prince of rappe-blankenburg
his residence in the metropolis render necessary by the duty entrust to he his high connection and the peculiar talent and nature of the man recommend he to person in power and dr proudie become know as a useful and rise clergyman
some few year since even within the memory of many who be not yet willing to call themselves old a liberal clergyman be a person not frequently to be meet
sydney smith be such and be look on as a little well than an infidel a few other also might be name but they be rarae ave and be regard with doubt and distrust by their brother
no man be so surely a tory as a country rector--nowhere be the power that be so cherished as at oxford
when however dr whately be make an archbishop and dr hampden some year afterwards regius professor many wise divine see that a change be take place in man s mind and that more liberal idea would henceforward be suitable to the priest as well as to the laity
clergymen begin to be hear of who have cease to anathematise papist on the one hand or vilify dissenter on the other
it appear clear that high church principle as they be call be no long to be the sure claim to promotion with at any rate one section of statesman and dr proudie be one among those who early in life adapt himself to the view hold by the whig on most theological and religious subject
he bear with the idolatry of rome tolerate even the infidelity of socinianism and be hand and glove with the presbyterian synods of scotland and ulster
such a man at such a time be find to be useful and dr proudie s name begin to appear in the newspaper
he be make one of a commission who go over to ireland to arrange matter preparative to the working of the national board he become honorary secretary to another commission nominate to inquire into the revenue of cathedral chapter and have have something to do with both the regium donum and the maynooth grant
it must not be on this account be take as prove that dr proudie be a man of great mental power or even of much capacity for business for such quality have not be require in he
in the arrangement of those church reform with which he be connect the idea and original conception of the work to be do be generally furnish by the liberal statesman of the day and the labour of the detail be bear by official of a low rank
it be however think expedient that the name of some clergyman should appear in such matter and as dr proudie have become know as a tolerating divine great use of this sort be make of his name
if he do not do much active good he never do any harm he be amenable to those who be really in authority and at the sitting of the various board to which he belong maintain a kind of dignity which have its value
he be certainly possess of sufficient tact to answer the purpose for which he be require without make himself troublesome but it must not therefore be surmise that he doubt his own power or fail to believe that he could himself take a high part in high affair when his own turn come
his be bid his time and patiently look forward to the day when he himself would sit authoritative at some board and talk and direct and rule the roost while less star sit round and obey as he have so well accustom himself to do
his reward and his time have now come
he be select for the vacant bishopric and on the next vacancy which might occur in any diocese would take his place in the house of lords prepare to give not a silent vote in all matter concern the weal of the church establishment
toleration be to be the basis on which he be to fight his battle and in the honest courage of his heart he think no evil would come to he in encounter even such foe as his brother of exeter and oxford
dr proudie be an ambitious man and before he be well consecrated bishop of barchester he have begin to look up to archepiscopal splendour and the glory of lambeth or at any rate of bishopsthorpe
he be comparatively young and have as he fondly flatter himself be select as possess such gift natural and acquire as must be sure to recommend he to a yet high notice now that a high sphere be open to he
dr proudie be therefore quite prepared to take a conspicuous part in all theological affair appertain to these realm and have such view by no mean intend to bury himself at barchester as his predecessor have do
no london should still be his ground a comfortable mansion in a provincial city might be well enough for the dead month of the year
indeed dr proudie have always feel it necessary to his position to retire from london when other great and fashionable people do so but london should still be his fix residence and it be in london that he resolve to exercise that hospitality so peculiarly recommend to all bishop by st paul
how otherwise could he keep himself before the world
how else give the government in matter theological the full benefit of his weight and talent
this resolution be no doubt a salutary one as regard the world at large but be not likely to make he popular either with the clergy or the people of barchester
dr grantly have always live there and in truth it be hard for a bishop to be popular after dr grantly
his income have average l 9000 a year his successor be to be rigidly limit to l 5000
he have but one child on whom to spend his money dr proudie have seven or eight
he have be a man of few personal expense and they have be confine to the taste of a moderate gentleman but dr proudie have to maintain a position in fashionable society and have that to do with comparatively small mean
dr grantly have certainly keep his carriage as become a bishop but his carriage horse and coachman though they do very well for barchester would have be almost ridiculous at westminster
mrs proudie determine that her husband s equipage should not shame she and thing on which mrs proudie resolve be generally accomplish
from all this it be likely to result that dr proudie would not spend much money at barchester whereas his predecessor have deal with the tradesman of the city in a manner very much to their satisfaction
the grantlys father and son have spend their money like gentleman but it soon become whisper in barchester that dr proudie be not unacquainted with those prudent device by which the utmost show of wealth be produce from limited mean
in person dr proudie be a good-look man spruce and dapper and very tidy
he be somewhat below middle height be about five foot four but he make up for the inch which he want by the dignity with which he carry those which he have
it be no fault of his own if he have not a commanding eye for he study hard to assume it
his feature be well form though perhaps the sharpness of his nose may give to his face in the eye of some people an air of insignificance
if so it be greatly redeem by his mouth and chin of which he be justly proud
dr proudie may well be say to have be a fortunate man for he be not bear to wealth and he be now bishop of barchester but nevertheless he have his care
he have a large family of whom the three eld be daughter now all grow up and fit for fashionable life and he have a wife
it be not my intention to breathe a word against the character of mrs proudie but still i cannot think that with all her virtue she add much to her husband s happiness
the truth be that in matter domestic she rule supreme over her titular lord and rule with a rod of iron
nor be this all
thing domestic dr proudie might have abandon to she if not voluntarily yet willingly
but mrs proudie be not satisfied with such home dominion and stretch her power over all his movement and will not even abstain from thing spiritual
in fact the bishop be henpecke
the archdeacon s wife in her happy home at plumstead know how to assume the full privilege of her rank and express her own mind in become tone and place
but mrs grantly s sway if sway she have be easy and beneficent
she never sham her husband before the world she be a pattern of obedience her voice be never loud nor her look sharp doubtless she value power and have not unsuccessfully strive to acquire it but she know what should be the limit of woman s rule
not so mrs proudie
this lady be habitually authoritative to all but to her poor husband she be despotic
successful as have be his career in the eye of the world it would seem that in the eye of his wife he be never right
all hope of defend himself have long pass from he indeed he rarely even attempt self-justification and be aware that submission produce the near approach to peace which his own house can ever attain
mrs proudie have not be able to sit at the board and committee to which her husband have be call by the state nor as he often reflect can she make her voice hear in the house of lords
it may be that she will refuse to he permission to attend to this branch of a bishop s duty it may be that she will insist on his close attendance to his own closet
he have never whisper a word on the subject to living ear but he have already make his fix resolve
should such an attempt be make he will rebel
dog have turn against their master and even neapolitans against their ruler when oppression have be too severe
and dr proudie feel within himself that if the cord be draw too tight he also can muster courage and resist
the state of vassalage in which our bishop have be keep by his wife have not tend to exalt his character in the eye of his daughter who assume in address their father too much of that authority which be not properly belong at any rate to they
they be on the whole fine engage young lady
they be tall and robust like their mother whose high cheek bone and--we may say auburn hair they all inherit
they think somewhat too much of their grand uncle who have not hitherto return the compliment by think much of they
but now that their father be a bishop it be probable that family tie will be draw close
consider their connection with the church they entertain but few prejudice against the pleasure of the world and have certainly not distress their parent as too many english girl have lately do by any enthusiastic wish to devote themselves to the seclusion of a protestant nunnery
dr proudie s son be still at school
one other marked peculiarity in the character of the bishop s wife must be mention
though not averse to the society and manner of the world she be in her own way a religious woman and the form in which this tendency show itself in she be by a strict observance of the sabbatarian rule
dissipation and low dress during the week be under her control atone for by three service an evening sermon read by herself and a perfect abstinence from any cheer employment on sunday
unfortunately for those under her roof to whom the dissipation and low dress be not extend her servant namely and her husband the compensate strictness of the sabbath include all
woe betide the recreant housemaid who be find to have be listen to the honey of a sweetheart in the regent s park instead of the soul-stir evening discourse of mr slope
not only be she send adrift but she be so send with a character which leave she little hope of a decent place
woe betide the six-foot hero who escort mrs proudie to her pew in red plush breech if he slip away to the neighbour beer-shop instead of fall into the back seat appropriate to his use
mrs proudie have the eye of argus for such offender
occasional drunkenness in the week may be overlook for six foot on low wage be hardly to be procure if the moral be always keep at a high pitch but not even for the grandeur or economy will mrs proudie forgive a desecration of the sabbath
in such matter mrs proudie allow herself to be often guide by that eloquent preacher the rev
mr slope and as dr proudie be guide by his wife it necessarily follow that the eminent man we have name have obtain a good deal of control over dr proudie in matter concern religion
mr slope s only preferment have hitherto be that of reader and preacher in a london district church and on the consecration of his friend the new bishop he readily give this up to undertake the onerous but congenial duty of domestic chaplain to the bishop
mr slope however on his first introduction must not be bring before the public at the tail of a chapter
chapter iv the bishop s chaplain of the rev
mr slope s parentage i be not able to say much
i have hear it assert that he be lineally descend from that eminent physician who assist at the birth of mr t
shandy and that in early year he add an e to his name for the sake of euphony as other great man have do before he
if this be so i presume he be christen obadiah for that be his name in commemoration of the conflict in which his ancestor so distinguish himself
all my research on the subject have however fail in enable i to fix the date on which the family change its religion
he have be a sizar at cambridge and have there conduct himself at any rate successfully for in due process of time he be an ma have university pupil under his care
from thence he be transfer to london and become preacher at a new district church build on the confine of baker street
he be in this position when congenial idea on religious subject recommend he to mrs proudie and the intercourse have become close and confidential
have be thus familiarly throw among the misses proudie it be more than natural that some soft feeling than friendship should be engender
there have be some passage of love between he and the eld hope olivia but they have hitherto result in no favourable arrangement
in truth mr slope have make a declaration of affection afterwards withdraw it on find that the doctor have no immediate worldly fund with which to endow his child and it may easily be conceive that miss proudie after such an announcement on his part be not readily disposed to receive any further show of affection
on the appointment of dr proudie to the bishopric of barchester mr slope s view be in truth somewhat alter
bishop even though they be poor can provide for clerical child and mr slope begin to regret that he have not be more disinterested
he no soon hear the tiding of the doctor s elevation than he recommence his siege not violently indeed but respectfully and at a distance
olivia proudie however be a girl of spirit she have the blood of two peer in her vein and well still she have another lover on her book so mr slope sigh in vain and the pair soon find it convenient to establish a mutual bond of inveterate hatred
it may be think singular that mrs proudie s friendship for the young clergyman should remain firm after such an affair but to tell the truth she have know nothing of it
though very fond of mr slope herself she have never conceive the idea that either of her daughter would become so and remember that their high birth and social advantage expect for they match of a different sort
neither the gentleman nor the lady find it necessary to enlighten she
olivia s two sister have each know of the affair so have all the servant so have all the people live in the adjoining house on either side but mrs proudie have be keep in the dark
mr slope soon comfort himself with the reflection that as he have be select as chaplain to the bishop it would probably be in his power to get the good thing in the bishop s gift without trouble himself with the bishop s daughter and he find himself able to endure the pang of reject love
as he sit himself down in the railway carriage confront the bishop and mrs proudie as they start on their first journey to barchester he begin to form in his own mind a plan of his future life
he know well his patron s strong point but he know the weak one as well
he understand correctly enough to what attempt the new bishop s high spirit would soar and he rightly guess that public life would well suit the great man s taste than the small detail of diocesan duty
he therefore he mr slope would in effect be bishop of barchester
such be his resolve and to give mr slope his due he have both courage and spirit to bear he out in his resolution
he know that he should have a hard battle to fight for the power and patronage of the see would be equally covet by another great mind--mrs proudie would also choose to be bishop of barchester
mr slope however flatter himself that he could outmanoeuvre the lady
she must live much in london while he would always be on the spot
she would necessarily remain ignorant of much while he would know everything belong to the diocese
at first doubtless he must flatter and cajole perhaps yield in some thing but he do not doubt of ultimate triumph
if all other mean fail he could join the bishop against the wife inspire courage into the unhappy man lie an axe to the rock of the woman s power and emancipate the husband
such be his thought as he sit look at the sleeping pair in the railway carriage and mr slope be not the man to trouble himself with such thought for nothing
he be possess of more than average ability and be of good courage
though he can stoop to fawn and stoop low indeed if need be he have still within he the power to assume the tyrant and with the power he have certainly the wish
his acquirement be not of the high order but such as they be they be completely under control and he know the use of they
he be gift with a certain kind of pulpit eloquence not likely indeed to be persuasive with man but powerful with the soft sex
in his sermon he deal greatly in denunciation excite the mind of his weak hearer with a not unpleasant terror and leave an impression on their mind that all mankind be in a perilous state and all womankind too except those who attend regularly to the evening lecture in baker street
his look and tone be extremely severe so much so that one cannot but fancy that he regard the great part of the world as be infinitely too bad for his care
as he walk through the street his very face denote his horror of the world s wickedness and there be always an anathema lurk in the corner of his eye
in doctrine he like his patron be tolerant of dissent if so strict a mind can be call tolerant of anything
with wesleyan-methodists he have something in common but his soul tremble in agony at the iniquity of the puseyites
his aversion be carry to thing outward as well as inward
his gall rise at a new church with a high pitch roof a full-breast black silk waistcoat be with he a symbol of satan and a profane jest-book would not in his view more foully desecrate the church seat of a christian than a book of prayer print with red letter and ornament with a cross on the back
most active clergyman have their hobby and sunday observance be his
sunday however be a word which never pollute his mouth--it be always the sabbath
the desecration of the sabbath as he delight to call it be to he meat and drink he thrive upon that as policeman do on the general evil habit of the community
it be the loved subject of all his evening discourse the source of all his eloquence the secret of his power over the female heart
to he the revelation of god appear in that one law give for jewish observance
to he the mercy of our saviour speak in vain to he in vain have be preach that sermon that fall from the divine lip on the mountain-- blessed be the meek for they shall inherit the earth blesse be the merciful for the they shall obtain mercy
to he the new testament be comparatively of little moment for from it can he draw no fresh authority for that dominion which he love to exercise over at least a seventh part of man s allotted time here below
mr slope be tall and not ill make
his foot and hand be large as have ever be the case with all his family but he have a broad chest and wide shoulder to carry off these excrescence and on the whole his figure be good
his countenance however be not specially prepossess
his hair be lank and of a dull pale reddish hue
it be always form into three straight lumpy mass each brush with admirable precision and cement with much grease two of they adhere closely to the side of his face and the other lie at right angle above they
he wear no whisker and be always punctiliously shaven
his face be nearly of the same colour as his hair though perhaps a little redder it be not unlike beef beef however one would say of a bad quality
his forehead be capacious and high but square and heavy and unpleasantly shin
his mouth be large though his lip be thin and bloodless and his big prominent pale brown eye inspire anything but confidence
his nose however be his redeem feature it be pronounce straight and well-form though i myself should have like it well if it do not possess a somewhat spongy porous appearance as though it have be cleverly form out of a red coloured cork
i never could endure to shake hand with mr slope
a cold clammy perspiration always exude from he the small drop be ever to be see stand on his brow and his friendly grasp be unpleasant
such be mr slope--such be the man who have suddenly fall into the midst of barchester close and be destine there to assume the station which have heretofore be fill by the son of the late bishop
think oh my meditative reader what an associate we have here for those comfortable prebendary those gentlemanlike clerical doctor those happy well-use well-feed minor canon who have grow into existence at barchester under the kindly wing of bishop grantly
but not as a mere associate for those do mr slope travel down to barchester with the bishop and his wife
he intend to be if not their master at least the chief among they
he intend to lead and to have follower he intend to hold the purse string of the diocese and draw round he an obedient herd of his poor and hungry brother
and here we can hardly fail to draw a comparison between the archdeacon and our new private chaplain and despite the manifold fault of the former one can hardly fail to make it much to his advantage
both man be eager much too eager to support and increase the power of their order
both be anxious that the world should be priest-govern though they have probably never confess as much even to themselves
both begrudge any other kind of dominion hold by man over man
dr grantly if he admit the queen s supremacy in thing spiritual only admit it as be due to the quasi priesthood convey on the consecrating quality of her coronation and he regard thing temporal as be by their nature subject to those which be spiritual
mr slope s idea of sacerdotal rule be of a quite different class
he care nothing one way or the other for the queen s supremacy these to his ear be empty word mean nothing
form he regard but little and such titular expression of supremacy consecration ordination and the like convey of themselves no significance to he
let he be supreme who can
the temporal king judge or gaoler can work but on the body
the spiritual master if he have the necessary gift and can duly use they have a wide field of empire
he work upon the soul
if he can make himself be believe he can be all powerful over those who listen
if he be careful to meddle with none who be too strong in intellect or too weak in flesh he may indeed be supreme
and such be the ambition of mr slope
dr grantly interfere very little with the worldly doing of those who be in any way subject to he
i do not mean to say that he omit to notice misconduct among his clergy immorality in his parish or omission in his family but he be not anxious to do so where the necessity could be avoid
he be not trouble with a propensity to be curious and as long as those around he be taint with no heretical lean towards dissent as long as they fully and freely admit the efficacy of mother church he be willing that that mother should be merciful and affectionate prone to indulgence and unwilling to chastise
he himself enjoy the good thing of this world and like to let it be know that he do so
he cordially despise any brother rector who think harm of dinner-party or dread the danger of a moderate claret-jug consequently dinner-party and claret-jug be common in the diocese
he like to give law and to be obey in they implicitly but he endeavour that his ordinance should be within the compass of the man and not unpalatable to the gentleman
he have rule among his clerical neighbour now for sundry year and as he have maintain his power without become unpopular it may be presume that he have exercise some wisdom
of mr slope s conduct much cannot be say as his grand career be yet to commence but it may be presume that his taste will be very different from those of the archdeacon
he conceive it to be his duty to know all the private doing and desire of the flock entrust to his care
from the poor class he exact and unconditional obedience to set rule of conduct and if disobey he have recourse like his great ancestor to the fulmination of an ernulfus thou shalt be damn in thy go in and in thy come out--in thy eating and thy drinking c c c
with the rich experience have already teach he a different line of action be necessary
man in the upper walk of life do not mind be curse and the woman presume that it be do in delicate phrase rather like it
but he have not therefore give up so important a portion of believe christians
with the man indeed he be generally at variance they be harden sinner on whom the voice of priestly charmer often fall in vain but with the lady old and young firm and frail devout and dissipate he be as he conceive all powerful
he can reprove fault with so much flattery and utter censure in so caress a manner that the female heart if it glow with a spark of low church susceptibility cannot withstand he
in many house he be thus an admired guest the husband for their wife sake be fain to admit he and when once admit it be not easy to shake he off
he have however a paw greasy way with he which do not endear he to those who do not value he for their soul sake and he be not a man to make himself at once popular in a large circle such as be now likely to surround he at barchester
chapter v a morning visit it be know that dr proudie would immediately have to reappoint to the wardenship of the hospital under the act of parliament to which allusion have be make but no one imagine that any choice be leave to he--no one for a moment thought that he could appoint any other than mr harding

such be the state of mr harding s mind in the matter he do not feel any peculiar personal interest in the appointment of dr proudie to the bishopric

he be not exactly the man to overlook his own slight claim or to forgive the preference show to another

do you reside in barchester dr grantly ask the lady with the sweetest smile

be the arrangement with reference to the sabbath-day school generally pretty good in your archdeaconry sabbath-day school repeat the archdeacon with an affectation of surprise

there be none at plumstead this be almost a fib on the part of the archdeacon for mrs grantly have a very nice school
to be sure it be not a sunday school exclusively and be not so designated but that exemplary lady always attend there an hour before church and hear the child say their catechism and see that they be clean and tidy for church with their hand wash and their shoe tie and grisel and florinda her daughter carry thither a basket of large bun bake on the saturday afternoon and distribute they to all the child not especially under disgrace which bun be carry home after church with considerable content and eat hot at tea be then split and toast
the child of plumstead would indeed open their eye if they hear their venerate pastor declare that there be no sunday school in the parish

hitherto barchester have escape the taint of any extreme rigour of church doctrine
the clergyman of the city and the neighbourhood though very well inclined to promote high-church principle privilege and prerogative have never commit themselves to tendency which be somewhat too loosely call puseyite practice

mr slope in commence his sermon show no slight tact in his ambiguous manner of hint that humble as he be himself he stand there as the mouthpiece of the illustrious divine who sit opposite to he and have presume so much he give forth a very accurate definition of the conduct which that prelate would rejoice to see in the clergyman now bring under his jurisdiction

what

all however agree in say that mr slope be to be debar from open his mouth in the cathedral of barchester many believe that the verger be to be order to refuse he even the accommodation of a seat and some of the most far-go advocate for strong measure declare that this sermon be look upon as an indictable offence and that proceeding be to be take against he for brawl
the party who be inclined to he--the enthusiastically religious young lady and the middle-aged spinster desirous of a move--of course take up his defence the more warmly on account of this attack
if they could not hear mr slope in the cathedral they would hear he elsewhere they would leave the dull dean the dull old prebendary and the scarcely less dull young minor canon to preach to each other they would work slipper and cushion and hem band for mr slope make he a happy martyr and stick he up in some new sion sic or bethesda and put the cathedral quite out of fashion
dr and mrs proudie at once return to london
they think it expedient not to have to encounter any personal application from the dean and chapter respect the sermon till the violence of the storm have expend itself but they leave mr slope behind they nothing daunt and he go about his work zealously flattering such as would listen to his flattery whisper religious twaddle into the ear of foolish woman ingratiate himself with the very few clergy who would receive he visit the house of the poor inquire into all people pry into everything and search with the minute eye into all palatial dilapidation
he do not however make any immediate attempt to preach again in the cathedral
and so all barchester be by the ear
chapter viii the ex-warden rejoices in his probable return to the hospital among the lady in barchester who have hitherto acknowledge mr slope as their spiritual director must not be reckon either the widow bold or her sister-in-law
