chapter ix the stanhope family it be now three month since dr proudie begin his reign and change have already be affect in the diocese which show at least the energy of an active mind
among other thing absentee clergyman have be favour with hint much too strong to be overlook
poor dear old bishop grantly have on this matter be too lenient and the archdeacon have never be incline to be severe with those who be absent on reputable pretence and who provide for their duty in a liberal way
among the great of the diocesan sinner in this respect be dr vesey stanhope
year have now pass since he have do a day s duty and yet there be no reason against his do duty except a want of inclination on his own part
he hold a prebendal stall in the diocese one of the good residence in the close and the two large rectory of crabtree canonicorum and stogpingum
indeed he have the cure of three parish for that of eiderdown be join to stogpingum
he have reside in italy for twelve year
his first go there have be attribute to a sore throat and that sore throat though never repeat in any violent manner have stand he in such stead that it have enable he to live in easy idleness ever since
he have now be summon home not indeed with rough violence or by any peremptory command but by a mandate which he find himself unable to disregard
mr slope have write to he by the bishop s desire
in the first place the bishop much want the valuable co-operation of dr vesey stanhope in the diocese in the next the bishop think it his imperative duty to become personally acquaint with the most conspicuous of his diocesan clergy then the bishop think it essentially necessary for dr stanhope s own interest that dr stanhope should at any rate for a time return to barchester and lastly it be say that so strong a feeling be at the present moment evince by the hierarch of the church with reference to the absence of its clerical member that it behove dr vesey stanhope not to allow his name to stand among those which would probably in a few month be submit to the council of the nation
there be something so ambiguously frightful in this last threat that dr stanhope determine to spend two or three summer month at his residence in barchester
his rectory be inhabit by his curate and he feel himself from disuse to be unfit for parochial duty but his prebendal home be keep empty for he and he think it probable that he might be able now and again to preach a prebendal sermon
he arrive therefore with all his family at barchester and he and they must be introduce to my reader
the great family characteristic of the stanhopes might probably be say to be heartlessness but the want of feeling be in most of they accompany by so great an amount of good nature that their neighbour fail to perceive how indifferent to they be the happiness and well-being of those around they
the stanhopes would visit you in your sickness provide it be not contagious would bring you orange french novel and the last new bit of scandal and then hear of your death or your recovery with an equally indifferent composure
their conduct to each other be the same as to the world they bore and forbore and there be sometimes as will be see much necessity for forbearing but their love among themselves rarely reach above this
it be astonish how much each of the family be able to do and how much each do to prevent the well-being of the other four
for there be five in all the doctor namely and mrs stanhope two daughter and one son
the doctor perhaps be the least singular and most estimable of they all and yet such good quality as he possess be all negative
he be a good look rather plethoric gentleman of about sixty year of age
his hair be snow white very plentiful and somewhat like wool of the fine description
his whisker be large and very white and give to his face the appearance of a benevolent sleepy old lion
his dress be always unexceptionable
although he have live so many year in italy it be invariably of a decent clerical hue but it never be hyperclerical
he be a man not give to much talking but what little he do say be generally well say
his reading seldom go beyond romance and poetry of the light and not always most moral description
he be thoroughly a bon vivant an accomplish judge of wine though he never drink to excess and a most inexorable critic in all affair touch the kitchen
he have have much to forgive in his own family since a family have grow up around he and have forgive everything--except inattention to his dinner
his weakness in that respect be now fully understand and his temper but seldom try
as dr stanhope be a clergyman it may be suppose that his religious conviction make up a considerable part of his character but this be not so
that he have religious conviction must be believe but he rarely obtrude they even on his child
this abstinence on his part be not systematic but very characteristic of the man
it be not that he have predetermine never to influence their thought but he be so habitually idle that his time for do so have never come till the opportunity for do so be go forever
whatever conviction the father may have have the child be at any rate but indifferent member of the church from which he draw his income
such be dr stanhope
the feature of mrs stanhope s character be even less plainly marked than those of her lord
the far niente of her italian life have enter into her very soul and bring she to regard a state of inactivity as the only earthly good
in manner and appearance she be exceedingly prepossess
she have be a beauty and even now at fifty-five she be a handsome woman
her dress be always perfect she never dress but once in the day and never appear till between three and four but when she do appear she appear at her good
whether the toil rest partly with she or wholly with she handmaid it be not for such a one as the author to imagine
the structure of her attire be always elaborate and yet never over laboured
she be rich in apparel but not bedizen with finery her ornament be costly rare and such as could not fail to attract notice but they do not look as though wear with that purpose
she well know the great architectural secret of decorate her construction and never condescend to construct a decoration
but when we have say that mrs stanhope know how to dress and use her knowledge daily we have say all
other purpose in life she have none
it be something indeed that she do not interfere with the purpose of other
in early life she have undergo great trial with reference to the doctor s dinner but for the last ten or twelve year her eld daughter charlotte have take that labour off her hand and she have have little to trouble her little that is till the edict for this terrible english journey have go forth since then indeed her life have be laborious enough
for such a one the toil of be carry from the shore of como to the city of barchester be more than labour enough let the care of the carrier be ever so vigilant
mrs stanhope have be oblige to have every one of her dress take in from the effect of the journey
charlotte stanhope be at this time about thirty-five year old and whatever may have be her fault she have none of those which belong particularly to old young lady
she neither dress young nor talk young nor indeed look young
she appear to be perfectly content with her time of life and in no way affect the grace of youth
she be a fine young woman and have she be a man would have be a very fine young man
all that be do in the house and that be not do by servant be do by she
she give the order pay the bill hire and dismiss the domestic make the tea carve the meat and manage everything in the stanhope household
she and she alone could ever induce her father to look into the state of his worldly concern
she and she alone could in any degree control the absurdity of her sister
she and she alone prevent the whole family from fall into utter disrepute and beggary
it be by her advice that they now find themselves very unpleasantly situate in barchester
so far the character of charlotte stanhope be not unprepossesse
but it remain to be say that the influence which she have in her family though it have be use to a certain extent for their worldly well-being have not be use to their real benefit as it might have be
she have aid her father in his indifference to his professional duty counsel he that his living be as much as his individual property as the estate of his eld brother be the property of that worthy peer
she have for year past stifle every little rise wish for a return to england which the doctor have from time to time express
she have encourage she mother in her idleness in order that she herself might be mistress and manager of the stanhope household
she have encourage and foster the folly of her sister though she be always willing and often able to protect she from their probable result
she have do she good and have thoroughly succeed in spoil she brother and turn he loose upon the world an idle man without a profession and without a shilling that he could call his own
miss stanhope be a clever woman able to talk on most subject and quite indifferent as to what the subject be
she pride herself on her freedom from english prejudice and she might have add from feminine delicacy
on religion she be a pure freethinker and with much want of true affection delight to throw out her own view before the troubled mind of her father
to have shake what remain of his church of england faith would have gratify she much but the idea of his abandon his preferment in the church have never once present itself to her mind
how could he indeed when he have no income from any other source
but the two most prominent member of the family still remain to be describe
the second child have be christen madeline and have be a great beauty
we need not say have be for she be never more beautiful than at the time of which we write though her person for many year have be disfigure by an accident
it be unnecessary that we should give in detail the early history of madeline stanhope
she have go to italy when seventeen year of age and have be allow to make the most of her surpass beauty in the saloon of milan and among the crowded villa along the shore of the lake of como
she have become famous for adventure in which her character be just not lose and have destroy the heart of a dozen cavalier without once be touch in her own
blood have flow in quarrel about her charm and she hear of these encounter with pleasurable excitement
it have be tell of she that on one occasion she have stand by in the disguise of a page and have see her lover fall
as be so often the case she have marry the very bad of those who seek her hand
why she have choose paulo neroni a man of no birth and no property a mere captain in the pope s guard one who have come up to milan either simply as an adventurer or as a spy a man of harsh temper and oily manner mean in figure swarthy in face and so false in word as to be hourly detect need not now be tell
when the moment for do so come she have probably no alternative
he at any rate have become her husband and after a prolong honeymoon among the lake they have go together to rome the papal captain have vainly endeavour to induce his wife to remain behind he
six month afterwards she arrive at her father s house a cripple and a mother
she have arrive without even notice with hardly clothe to cover she and without one of those many ornament which have grace her bridal trousseaux
her baby be in the arm of a poor girl from milan whom she have take in exchange for the roman maid who have accompany she thus far and who have then as she mistress say become homesick and have return
it be clear that the lady have determine that there should be no witness to tell story of her life in rome
she have fall she say in ascend a ruin and have fatally injure the sinew of her knee so fatally that when she stand she lose eight inch of her accustomed height so fatally that when she essay to move she could only drag herself painfully along with protrude hip and extended foot in a manner less graceful than that of a hunchback
she have consequently make up her mind once and for ever that she would never stand and never attempt to move herself
story be not slow to follow she aver that she have be cruelly ill-use by neroni and that to his violence have she owe her accident
be that as it may little have be say about her husband but that little have make it clearly intelligible to the family that signor neroni be to be see and hear of no more
there be no question as to re-admit the poor ill-use beauty to her old family right no question as to adopt her infant daughter beneath the stanhope roof tree
though heartless the stanhopes be not selfish
the two be take in pet make much of for a time all but adore and then feel by the two parent to be great nuisance in the house
but in the house the lady be and there she remain have her own way though that way be not very comfortable with the customary usage of an english clergyman
madame neroni though force to give up all motion in the world have no intention whatever of give up the world itself
the beauty of her face be uninjured and that beauty be of a peculiar kind
her copious rich brown hair be wear in grecian bandeaux round her head display as much as possible of her forehead and cheek
her forehead though rather low be very beautiful from its perfect contour and pearly whiteness
her eye be long and large and marvellously bright might i venture to say bright as lucifer s i should perhaps well express the depth of their brilliancy
they be dreadful eye to look at such as would absolutely deter any man of quiet mind and easy spirit from attempt a passage of arm with such foe
there be talent in they and the fire of passion and the play of wit but there be no love
cruelty be there instead and courage a desire for masterhood cunning and a wish for mischief
and yet as eye they be very beautiful
the eyelash be long and perfect and the long steady unabashed gaze with which she would look into the face of her admirer fascinated while it frighten he
she be a basilisk from whom an ardent lover of beauty could make no escape
her nose and mouth more so at twenty-eight than they have be at eighteen
what wonder that with such charm still glow in her face and with such deformity destroy her figure she should resolve to be see but only to be see recline on a sofa
her resolve have not be carry out without difficulty
she have still frequent the opera at milan she have still be see occasionally in the saloon of the noblesse she have cause herself to be carry in and out from her carriage and that in such a manner as in no wise to disturb her charm disarrange her dress or expose her deformity
her sister always accompany she and a maid a manservant also and on state occasion two
it be impossible that her purpose could have be achieve with less and yet poor as she be she have achieve her purpose
and then again the more dissolute italian youth of milan frequent the stanhope villa and surround her couch not greatly to her father s satisfaction
sometimes his spirit would rise a dark spot would show itself on his cheek and he would rebel but charlotte would assuage he with some peculiar triumph of her culinary art and all again would be smooth for a while
madeline affect all manner of rich and quaint device in the garniture of her room her person and her feminine belonging
in nothing be this more apparent than in the visit card which she have prepare for her use
for such an article one would say that she in her present state could have but small need see how improbable it be that she should make a morning call but not such be her own opinion
her card be surround by a deep border of gilding on this she have imprint in three lines la signora madeline vesey neroni
nata stanhope
and over the name she have a bright gilt coronet which certainly look very magnificent
how she have come to concoct such a name for herself it would be difficult to explain
her father have be christen vesey as another man be christen thomas and she have no more right to assume it than would have the daughter of a mr josiah jones to call herself mrs josiah smith on marry a man of the latter name
the gold coronet be equally out of place and perhaps insert with even less excuse
paul neroni have not the faint title to call himself a scion of even italian nobility
have the pair meet in england neroni would probably have be a count but they have meet in italy and any such pretence on his part would have be simply ridiculous
a coronet however be a pretty ornament and if it could solace a poor cripple to have such on her card who could begrudge it to she
of her husband or of his individual family she never speak but with her admirer she would often allude in a mysterious way to she married life and isolated state and point to her daughter would call she the last of the blood of the emperor thus refer neroni s extraction to the old roman family from which the bad of the caesars sprang
the signora be not without talent and not without a certain sort of industry she be an indomitable letter writer and her letter be worth the postage they be full of wit mischief satire love latitudinarian philosophy free religion and sometimes alas
loose ribaldry
the subject however depend entirely on the recipient and she be prepared to correspond with any one but moral young lady or stiff old woman
she write also a kind of poetry generally in italian and short romance generally in french
she read much of a desultory sort of literature and as a modern linguist have really make great proficiency
such be the lady who have now come to wound the heart of the man of barchester
ethelbert stanhope be in some respect like his young sister but he be less inestimable as a man than she be as a woman
his great fault be an entire absence of that principle which should have induce he as the son of a man without fortune to earn his own bread
many attempt have be make to get he to do so but these have all be frustrate not so much by idleness on his part as by a disinclination to exert himself in any way not to his taste
he have be educate at eton and have be intend for the church but have leave cambridge in disgust after a single term and notify to his father his intention to study for the bar
preparatory to that he think it well that he should attend a german university and consequently go to leipzig
there he remain two year and bring away a knowledge of german and a taste for the fine art
he still however intend himself for the bar take chamber engage himself to sit at the foot of a learn pundit and spend a season in london
he there find that all his aptitude incline he to the life of an artist and he determine to live by paint
with this object he retire to milan and have himself rig out for rome
as a painter he might have earn his bread for he want only diligence to excel but when at rome his mind be carry away by other thing he soon write home for money say that he have be convert to the mother church that he be already an acolyte of the jesuits and that he be about to start with other to palestine on a mission for convert jews
he do go to judea but be unable to convert the jews be convert by they
he again write home to say that moses be the only giver of perfect law to the world that the coming of the true messiah be at hand that great thing be do in palestine and that he have meet one of the family of sidonis a most remarkable man who be now on his way to western europe and whom he have induce to deviate from his route with the object of call at the stanhope villa
ethelbert then express his hope that his mother and sister would listen to this wonderful prophet
his father he know could not do so from pecuniary consideration
this sidonia however do not take so strong a fancy to he as another of that family once do to a young english nobleman
at least he provide he with no hope of gold as large as lion so that the judaised ethelbert be again oblige to draw on the revenue of the christian church
it be needless to tell how the father swear that he would send no more money and receive no jew nor how charlotte declare that ethelbert could not be leave penniless in jerusalem and how la signora neroni resolve to have sidonia at her foot
the money be send and the jew do come
the jew do come but he be not at all to the taste of la signora
he be a dirty little old man and though he have provide no golden lion he have it seem relieve young stanhope s necessity
he positively refuse to leave the villa till he get a bill from the doctor on his london banker
ethelbert do not long remain a jew
he soon reappear at the villa without prejudice on the subject of his religion and with a firm resolve to achieve fame and fortune as a sculptor
he bring with he some model which he have originate at rome and which really give much fair promise that his father be induce to go to further expense in further these view
ethelbert open an establishment or rather take lodging and workshop at carrara and there spoil much marble and make some few pretty image
since that period now four year ago he have alternate between carrara and the villa but his sojourn at the workshop become short and short and those at the villa long and long
twas no wonder for carrara be not a spot in which an englishman would like to dwell
when the family start for england he have resolve not to be leave behind and with the assistance of his eld sister have earn his point against his father s wish
it be necessary he say that he should come to england for order
how otherwise be he to bring his profession to account
in personal appearance ethelbert stanhope be the most singular of being
he be certainly very handsome
he have his sister madeline s eye without their stare and without their hard cunning cruel firmness
they be also very much light and of so light and clear a blue as to make his face remarkable if nothing else do so
on enter a room with he ethelbert s blue eye would be the first thing you would see and on leave it almost the last thing you would forget
his light hair be very long and silky come down over his coat
his beard have be prepare in the holy land and be patriarchal
he never shave and rarely trim it
it be glossy soft clean and altogether not unprepossesse
it be such that lady might desire to reel it off and work it into their pattern in lieu of floss silk
his complexion be fair and almost pink he be small in height and slender in limb but well-make and his voice be of particular sweetness manner and dress he be equally remarkable
he have none of the mauvaise honte of an englishman
he require no introduction to make himself agreeable to any person
he habitually address stranger lady as well as man without any such formality and in do so never seem to meet with rebuke
his costume cannot be describe because it be so various but it be always totally opposed in every principle of colour and construction to the dress of those with whom he for the time consort
he be habitually addict to make love to lady and do so without scruple of conscience or any idea that such a practice be amiss
he have no heart to touch himself and be literally unaware that humanity be subject to such infliction
he have not think much about it but have he be ask would have say that ill-treat a lady s heart mean injure her promotion in the world
his principle therefore forbid he to pay attention to a girl if he think any man be present whom it might suit she to marry
in this manner his good nature frequently interfere with his amusement but he have no other motive in abstain from the full declaration of love to every girl that please his eye
bertie stanhope as he be generally call be however popular with both sex and with italians as well as english
his circle of acquaintance be very large and embrace people of all sort
he have not respect for rank and no aversion to those below he
he have live on familiar term with english peer german shopkeeper and roman priest
all people be nearly alike to he
he be above or rather below all prejudice
no virtue could charm he no vice shock he
he have about he a natural good manner which seem to qualify he for the high circle and yet he be never out of place in the low
he have no principle no regard for other no self-respect no desire to be other than a drone in a hive if only he could as a drone get what honey be sufficient for he
of honey in his latter day it may probably be presage that he will have but short allowance
such be the family of the stanhopes who at this period suddenly join themselves to the ecclesiastical circle of barchester close
any stranger union it would be impossible perhaps to conceive
and it be not as though they all fall down into the cathedral precinct hitherto unknown and untalked of
in such case no amalgamation would have be at all probable between the new comer and either the proudie set or the grantly set
but such be far from be the case
the stanhopes be all know by name in barchester and barchester be prepared to receive they with open arm
the doctor be one of the prebendary one of her rector one of her pillar of strength and be moreover count on as a sure ally both by proudies and grantlys
he himself be the brother of one peer and his wife be the sister of another--and both these peer be lord of whiggish tendency with whom the new bishop have some sort of alliance
this be sufficient to give to mr slope high hope that he might enlist dr stanhope on his side before his enemy could out-manoeuvre he
on the other hand the old dean have many many year ago in the day of the doctor s clerical energy be instrumental in assist he in his view as to preferment and many many year ago also the two doctor stanhope and grantly have as young parson be joyous together in the common room of oxford
dr grantly consequently do not doubt but that the new comer would range himself under his banner
little do any of they dream of what ingredient the stanhope family be now compose
chapter x mrs proudie s reception--commenced the bishop and his wife have only spend three or four day in barchester on the occasion of their first visit
his lordship have as we have see take his seat on his throne but his demeanour there into which it have be his intention to infuse much hierarchical dignity have be a good deal disarrange by the audacity of his chaplain s sermon
he have hardly dare to look his clergy in the face and to declare by the severity of his countenance that in truth he mean all that his factotum be say on his behalf nor yet do he dare throw mr slope over and show to those around he that he be no party to the sermon and would resent it
he have accordingly bless his people in a shambling manner not at all to his own satisfaction and have walk back to his palace with his mind very doubtful as to what he would say to his chaplain on the subject
he do not remain long in doubt
he have hardly doff his lawn when the partner of all his toil enter his study and exclaim even before she have seat herself-- bishop do you ever hear a more sublime more spirit-move more appropriate discourse than that well my love ha-hum-he the bishop do not know what to say
i hope my lord you donot mean to say you disapprove there be a look about the lady s eye which do not admit of my lord s disapprove at that moment
he feel that if he intend to disapprove it must be now or never but he also feel that it could not be now
it be not in he to say to the wife of his bosom that mr slope s sermon be ill-time impertinent and vexatious
no no reply the bishop
no i cannot say i disapprove--a very clever sermon and very well intend and i dare say will do a great deal of good this last praise be add see that what he have already say by no mean satisfy mrs proudie
i hope it will say she
i be sure it be well deserve
do you ever in your life bishop hear anything so like play-act as the way in which mr harding sing the litany
i shall beg mr slope to continue a course of sermon on the subject till all that be alter
we will have at any rate in our cathedral a decent godly modest morning service
there must be no more play-act here now and so the lady ring for lunch
this bishop know more about cathedral and dean and precentor and church service than his wife do and also more of the bishop s power
but he think it well at present to let the subject drop
my dear say he i think we must go back to london on tuesday
i find that my staying here will be very inconvenient to the government the bishop know that to this proposal his wife would not object and he also feel that by thus retreat from the ground of battle the heat of the fight might be get over in his absence
mr slope will remain here of course say the lady
oh of course say the bishop
thus after less than a week s sojourn in his palace do the bishop fly from barchester nor do he return to it for two month the london season be then over
during that time mr slope be not idle but he do not again assay to preach in the cathedral
in answer to mrs proudie s letter advise a course of sermon he have plead that he would at any rate wish to put off such an undertaking till she be there to hear they
he have employ his time in consolidate a proudie and slope party--or rather a slope and proudie party and he have not employ his time in vain
he do not meddle with the dean and chapter except by give they little teasing intimation of the bishop s wish about this and the bishop s feeling about that in a manner which be to they sufficiently annoying but which they could not resent
he preach once or twice in a distant church in the suburb of the city but make no allusion to the cathedral service
he commence the establishment of the bishop of barchester s sabbath-day school give notice of a propose bishop of barchester young men s sabbath evening lecture room and write three or four letter to the manager of the barchester branch railway inform he how anxious the bishop be that the sunday train should be discontinue
at the end of two month however the bishop and the lady reappear and as a happy harbinger of their return herald their advent by the promise of an evening party on the large scale
the ticket of invitation be send out from london--they be date from bruton street and be dispatch by the odious sabbath-break railway in a huge brown paper parcel to mr slope
everybody call himself a gentleman or herself a lady within the city of barchester and a circle of two mile round it be include
ticket be send to all the diocesan clergy and also to many other person of priestly note of whose absence the bishop or at least the bishop s wife feel tolerably confident
it be intended however to be a thronged and noticeable affair and preparation be make for receive some hundred
and now there arise considerable agitation among the grantleyites whether or not they would attend the bidding
the first feeling with they all be to send the brief excuse both for themselves and their wife and daughter
but by degree policy prevail over passion
the archdeacon perceive that he would be make a false step if he allow the cathedral clergy to give the bishop just ground of umbrage
they all meet in conclave and agree to go
the old dean would crawl in if it be but for half an hour
the chancellor treasurer archdeacon prebendary and minor canon would all go and would take their wife
mr harding be especially bid to go resolve in his heart to keep himself remove from mrs proudie
and mrs bold be determined to go though assure by her father that there be no necessity for such a sacrifice on her part
when all barchester be to be there neither eleanor nor mary bold understand why they should stay away
have they not be invite separately
and have not a separate little note from the chaplain couch in the most respectful language be enclose with the huge episcopal card
and the stanhopes would be there one and all
even the lethargic mother would so far bestir herself on such an occasion
they have only just arrive
the card be at the residence wait for they
no one in barchester have see they and what well opportunity could they have of show themselves to the barchester world
some few old friend such as the archdeacon and his wife have call and have find the doctor and his eld daughter but the elite of the family be not yet know
the doctor indeed wish in his heart to prevent the signora from accept the bishop s invitation but she herself have fully determine that she would accept it
if her father be ashamed of have his daughter carry into a bishop s palace she have no such feeling
indeed i shall she say to her sister who have greatly endeavour to dissuade she by say that the company would consist wholly of parson and parson wife
parson i suppose be much the same as other man if you strip they of their black coat and as to their wife i dare say they willnot trouble i
you may tell papa i donot mean to be leave at home papa be tell and feel that he could do nothing but yield
he also feel that it be useless of he now to be ashamed of his child
such as they be they have become such under his auspex as he have make his bed so he must lie upon it as he have sow his seed so must he reap his corn
he do not indeed utter such reflection in such language but such be the gist of his thought
it be not because madeline be a cripple that he shrink from see she make one of the bishop s guest but because he know that she would practise her accustomed lur and behave herself in a way that could not fail of be distasteful to the propriety of englishwomen
these thing have annoy but not shock he in italy
there they have shock no one but here in barchester here among his fellow parson he be ashamed that they should be see
such have be his feeling but he repress they
what if his brother clergyman be shock
they could not take it from his preferment because the manner of his married daughter be too free
la signora neroni have at any rate no fear that she would shock anybody
her ambition be to create a sensation to have parson at her foot see that the manhood of barchester consist mainly of parson and to send if possible every parson s wife home with a green fit of jealousy
none could be too old for she and hardly any too young
none too sanctify and none too worldly
she be quite prepared to entrap the bishop himself and then to turn up her nose at the bishop s wife
she do not doubt of success for she have always succeed but one thing be absolutely necessary she must secure the entire use of a sofa
the card send to dr and mrs stanhope and family have be send in an envelope have on the cover mr slope s name
the signora soon learn that mrs proudie be not yet at the palace and that the chaplain be manage everything
it be much more in her line to apply to he than to the lady and she accordingly write to he the prettiest little billet in the world
in five line she explain everything declare how impossible it be for she not to be desirous to make the acquaintance of such person as the bishop of barchester and his wife and she might add also of mr slope depict her own grievous state and conclude by be assure that mrs proudie would forgive her extreme hardihood in petitioning to be allow to be carry to a sofa
she then enclose one of her beautiful card
in return she receive as polite an answer from mr slope--a sofa should be keep in the large drawing-room immediately at the top of the grand stair especially for her use
and now the day of the party have arrive
the bishop and his wife come down from town only on the morning of the eventful day as behove such great people to do but mr slope have toil day and night to see that everything should be in right order
there have be much to do
no company have be see in the palace since heaven know when
new furniture have be require new pot and pan new cup and saucer new dish and plate

so the supper be concede the guest however be to stand as they consume it
there be four room open into each other on the first floor of the house which be denominate the drawing-room the reception-room and mrs proudie s boudoir
in olden day one of these have be bishop grantly s bed-room and another his common sit-room and study
the present bishop however have be move down into a back parlour and have be give to understand that he could very well receive his clergy in the dining-room should they arrive in too large a flock to be admit to his small sanctum
he have be unwilling to yield but after a short debate have yield
mrs proudie s heart beat high as she inspect her suite of room
they be really very magnificent or at least would be so by candlelight and they have nevertheless be get up with commendable economy
large room when full of people and full of light look well because they be large and be full and be light
small room be those which require costly fitting and rich furniture
mrs proudie know this and make the most of it she have therefore a huge gas lamp with a dozen burner hang from each of the ceiling
people be to arrive at ten supper be to last from twelve to one and at half-past one everybody be to be go

they be desire to take up at a quarter before one
it be manage excellently and mr slope be invaluable

he well understand that curate and country vicar with their belonging do not require so generous an article as the dignitary of the close

chapter xi mrs proudie s reception--concluded bishop of barchester i presume say bertie stanhope put out his hand frankly i be delighted to make your acquaintance

chapter xii slope versus harding two or three day after the party mr harding receive a note beg he to call on mr slope at the palace at an early hour the follow morning
there be nothing uncivil in the communication and yet the tone of it be thoroughly displease
it be as follow my dear mr harding will you favour i by call on i at the palace to-morrow morning at 9 30 am
the bishop wish i to speak to you touch the hospital
i hope you will excuse my naming so early an hour
i do so as my time be greatly occupy
if however it be positively inconvenient to you i will change it to 10
you will perhaps be kind enough to give i a note in reply
believe i to be my dear mr harding your assured friend obh
slope the palace monday morning 20 th august 185 mr harding neither could nor would believe anything of the sort and he think moreover that mr slope be rather impertinent to call himself by such a name
his assured friend indeed
how many assure friend generally fall to the lot of a man in this world
and by what process be they make
and how much of such process have take place as yet between mr harding and mr slope
mr harding could not help ask himself these question as he read and re-read the note before he
he answer it as follow dear sir i will call at the palace to-morrow at 9 30 am as you desire
truly your s
harding and on the follow morning punctually at half-past nine he knock at the palace door and ask for mr slope
the bishop have one small room allot to he on the ground-floor and mr slope have another
into this latter mr harding be show and ask to sit down
mr slope be not yet there
the ex-warden stand up at the window look into the garden and could not help think how very short a time have pass since the whole of that house have be open to he as though he have be a child of the family bear and breed in it
he remember how the old servant use to smile as they open the door to he how the familiar butler would say when he have be absent for a few hour long than usual a sight of you mr harding be good for sore eye how the fussy housekeeper would swear that he couldnot have din or couldnot have breakfast or couldnot have lunch
and then above all he remember the pleasant gleam of inward satisfaction which always spread itself over the old bishop s face whenever his friend enter his room
a tear come into his eye as he reflect that all this be go
what use would the hospital be to he now
he be alone in the world and get old he would soon very soon have to go and leave it all as his dear old friend have gone go and leave the hospital and his accustomed place in the cathedral and his haunt and pleasure to young and perhaps wise man in truth the time for it have go by
he feel as though the world be sink from his foot as though this this be the time for he to turn with confidence to other
what say he to himself can a man s religion be worth if it do not support he against the natural melancholy of decline year and as he look out through his dim eye into the bright parterre of the bishop s garden he feel that he have the support which he want
nevertheless he do not like to be thus keep wait
if mr slope do not really wish to see he at half-past nine o clock why force he to come away from his lodging with his breakfast in his throat
to tell the truth it be policy on the part of mr slope
mr slope have make up his mind that mr harding should either accept the hospital with abject submission or else refuse it altogether and have calculate that he would probably be more quick to do the latter if he could be get to enter upon the subject in all ill-humour
perhaps mr slope be not altogether wrong in his calculation
it be nearly ten when mr slope hurry into the room and mutter something about the bishop and diocesan duty shake mr harding s hand ruthlessly and beg he to be seat
now the airy superiority which this man assume do go against the grain of mr harding and yet he do not know how to resent it
the whole tendency of his mind and disposition be oppose to any contra-assumption of grandeur on his own part and he havenot the worldly spirit or quickness necessary to put down insolent pretension by downright and open rebuke as the archdeacon would have do

about the hospital mr harding begin mr slope speak of it as the head of college at cambridge might speak of some sizarship which have to be dispose of

of course you know the income be very much reduced continue mr slope

i think on the whole the bishop be right for though the service require will not be of a very onerous nature they will be more so than they be before
and it be perhaps well that the clergy immediately attach to the cathedral town should be make comfortable to the extent of the ecclesiastical mean at our disposal will allow
those be the bishop s idea and i must say mine also mr harding sit rub one hand on the other but say not a word
so much for the income mr harding
the house will of course remain to the warden as before

and not only in barchester mr harding but in the world at large

the same thing be go on throughout the country
work be now require from every man who receive wage and they who have superintend the doing of the work and the paying of the wage be bind to see that this rule be carry out
new man mr harding be now need and be now forthcoming in the church as well as in other profession all this be wormwood to our old friend
he have never rat very high his own ability or activity but all the feeling of his heart be with the old clergy and any antipathy of which his heart be susceptible be direct against those new busy uncharitable self-laud man of whom mr slope be so good an example

the bishop be very anxious that you should accept the appointment but he wish you should understand beforehand what will be the required duty
in the first place a sabbath-day school will be attach to the hospital what
for the old man ask mr harding
no mr harding not for the old man but for the benefit of the child of such of the poor of barchester as it may suit

the bishop think that this will render it unnecessary that any separate seat in the cathedral should be reserve for the hospital inmate mr slope pause but mr harding still say nothing
indeed it would be difficult to find seat for the woman and on the whole mr harding i may as well say at once that for people of that class the cathedral service do not appear to i to be the most useful even if it be so for any class of people we will not discuss that if you please say mr harding
i be not desirous of do so at least not at the present moment
i hope however you fully understand the bishop s wish about the new establishment of the hospital and if as i do not doubt i shall receive from you an assurance that you will accord with his lordship s view it will give i very great pleasure to be the bearer from his lordship to you of the presentation of the appointment but if i disagree with his lordship s view ask mr harding
but i hope you do not say mr slope
but if i do again ask the other
if such unfortunately should be the case which i can hardly conceive i presume your own feeling will dictate to you the propriety of decline the appointment but if i accept the appointment and yet disagree with the bishop what then this question rather bother mr slope
it be true that he have talk the matter over with the bishop and have receive a sort of authority for suggest to mr harding the propriety of a sunday school and certain hospital service but he have no authority for say that those proposition be to be make peremptory condition attach to the appointment
the bishop s idea have be that mr harding would of course consent and that the school would become like the rest of those new establishment in the city under the control of his wife and his chaplain
mr slope s idea have be more correct
he intend that mr harding should refuse the situation and that an ally of his own should get it but he have not conceive the possibility of mr harding openly accept the appointment and as openly reject the condition

i will take an early opportunity of see his lordship myself say mr harding
such an arrangement say mr slope will hardly give his lordship satisfaction
indeed it be impossible that the bishop should himself see every clergyman in the diocese on every subject of patronage that may arise

mr harding have become very angry and it be probable that he might commit himself
i really do not see what that have to do with the question
you cannot think that the bishop would be justify in allow you to regard as a sinecure a situation that require an active man merely because you have be employ for many year in the cathedral but it might induce the bishop to see i if i ask he to do so
i shall consult my friend in this matter mr slope but i mean to be guilty of no subterfuge you may tell the bishop that as i altogether disagree with his view about the hospital i shall decline the situation if i find that any such condition be attach to it as those you have suggest and so say mr harding take his hat and go his way
mr slope be content
he consider himself at liberty to accept mr harding s last speech as an absolute refusal of the appointment

chapter xiii the rubbish cart mr harding be not a happy man as he walk down the palace pathway and step out into the close

a man be sufficiently condemn if it can only be show that either in politic or religion he do not belong to some new school establish within the last score of year
he may then regard himself as rubbish and expect to be cart away
a man be nothing now unless he have within he a full appreciation of the new era an ear in which it would seem that neither honesty nor truth be very desirable but in which success be the only touchstone of merit
we must laugh at every thing that be establish
let the joke be ever so bad ever so untrue to the real principle of joking nevertheless we must laugh--or else beware the cart
we must talk think and live up to the spirit of the time and write up to it too if that cacoethe be upon we or else we be nought
new man and now measure long credit and few scruple great success and wonderful ruin such be now the taste of englishmen who know how to live
alas alas
under the circumstance mr harding could not but feel that he be an englishman who do not know how to live
this new doctrine of mr slope and the rubbish cart new at least at barchester sadly disturb his equanimity
the same thing be go on throughout the whole country work be now require from every man who receive wage and have he be live all his life receive wage and do no work

the school of man to whom he profess to belong the grantlys the gwynnes and the old high set of oxford divines be afflict with no such self-accusation as these which trouble mr harding
they as a rule be as satisfied with the wisdom and propriety of their own conduct as can be any mr slope or any dr proudie with his own
but unfortunately for himself mr harding have little of this self-reliance

alas alas
the evidence seem generally to go against he
he have profess to himself in the bishop s parlour that in these come source of the sorrow of the age in these fit of sad regret from which the latter year of few reflect man can be free religion would suffice to comfort he
yes religion could console he for the loss of any worldly good but be his religion of that active sort which would enable he so to repent of misspent year as to pass those that be leave to he in a spirit of hope for the future
and such repentance itself be it not a work of agony and of tear

why should he not make the twelve hundred a year his own
and if he do so would it not be well for he to have a father-in-law comfortably provide with the good thing of this world
would it not moreover be much more easy for he to gain his daughter if he do all in his power to forward his father s view
these question present themselves to he in a very forcible way and yet there be many point of doubt
if he resolve to restore to mr harding his former place he must take the necessary step for do so at once he must immediately talk over the bishop quarrel on the matter with mrs proudie whom he know he could not talk over and let mr quiverful know that he have be a little too precipitate as to mr harding s positive refusal
that he could effect all this he do not doubt but he do not wish to effect it for nothing
he do not wish to give way to mr harding and then be reject by the daughter
he do not wish to lose one influential friend before he have gain another
and thus he ride home meditate the many thing in his mind
it occur to he that mrs bold be sister-in-law to the archdeacon and that not even for twelve hundred a year would he submit to that imperious man
a rich wife be a great desideratum to he but success in his profession be still great there be moreover other rich woman who might be willing to become wife and after all this twelve hundred a year might when inquire into melt away into some small sum utterly beneath his notice
then also he remember that mrs bold have a son
another circumstance also much influence he though it be one which may almost be say to have influence he against his will
the vision of signora neroni be perpetually before his eye
it would be too much to say that mr slope be lose in love but yet he think and keep continually think that he have never see so beautiful a woman
he be a man whose nature be open to such impulse and the wile of the italianised charmer have be thoroughly successful in impose upon his thought
we will not talk of his heart not that he have no heart but because his heart have little to do with his present feeling
his taste have be please his eye charm and his vanity gratify
he have be dazzle by a sort of loveliness which he have never before see and have be catch by an easy free voluptuous manner which be perfectly new to he
he have never be so tempt before and the temptation be now irresistible
he have not own to himself that he care for this woman more than for other around he but yet he think often of the time when he might see she next and make almost unconsciously little cunning plan for see she frequently
he have call at dr stanhope s house the day after the bishop s party and then the warmth of his admiration have be feed with fresh fuel
if the signora have be kind in her manner and flattering in her speech when lie upon the bishop s sofa with the eye of so many on she she have be much more so in her mother s drawing-room with no one present but her sister to repress either her nature or her art
mr slope have thus leave she quite bewilder and could not willingly admit into his brain any scheme a part of which would be the necessity of abandon all further special relationship with this lady
and so he slowly ride along very meditative
and here the author must beg it to be remember that mr slope be not in all thing a bad man
his motif like those of most man be mixed and though his conduct be generally very different from that which we would wish to praise it be actuate perhaps as often as that of the majority of the world by a desire to do his duty
he believe in the religion which he teach harsh unpalatable uncharitable as that religion be
he believe those whom he wish to get under his hoof the grantlys and gwynnes of the church to be the enemy of that religion
he believe himself to be the pillar of strength destine to do great thing and with that subtle selfish ambiguous sophistry to which the mind of all man be so subject he have teach himself to think that in do much for the promotion of his own interest he be do much also for the promotion of religion
mr slope have never be an immoral man
indeed he have resist temptation to immorality with a strength of purpose that be creditable to he
he have early in life devote himself to work which be not compatible with the ordinary pleasure of youth and he have abandon such pleasure not without a struggle
it must therefore be conceive that he do not admit to himself that he warmly admire the beauty of a married woman without heartfelt sting of conscience and to pacify that conscience he have to teach himself that the nature of his admiration be innocent
and thus he ride along meditative and ill at ease
his conscience have not a word to say against his choose the widow and her fortune
that he look upon as a godly work rather than otherwise as a deed which if carry through would redound to his credit as a christian
on that side lie no future remorse no conduct which he might probably have to forget no inward sting
if it should turn out to be really the fact that mrs bold have twelve hundred a year at her own disposal mr slope would rather look upon it as a duty which he owe his religion to make himself the master of the wife and the money as a duty too in which some amount of self-sacrifice would be necessary
he would have to give up his friendship with the signora his resistance to mr harding his antipathy--no he find on mature self-examination that he could not bring himself to give up his antipathy to dr grantly
he would marry the lady as the enemy of her brother-in-law if such an arrangement suit she if not she must look elsewhere for a husband
it be with such resolve as this that he reach barchester
he would at once ascertain what the truth might be as to the lady s wealth and have do this he would be rule by circumstance in his conduct respect the hospital
if he find that he could turn round and secure the place for mr harding without much self-sacrifice he would do so but if not he would woo the daughter in opposition to the father
but in no case would he succumb to the archdeacon
he see his horse take round to the stable and immediately go forth to commence his inquiry
to give mr slope his due he be not a man who ever let much grass grow under his foot
poor eleanor
she be doom to be the intended victim of more scheme than one
about the time that mr slope be visit the vicar of puddingdale a discussion take place respect her charm and wealth at dr stanhope s house in the close
there have be morning caller there and people have tell some truth and also some falsehood respect the property which john bold have leave behind he
by degree the visitor go and as the doctor go with they and as the doctor s wife have not make her appearance charlotte stanhope and her brother be leave together
he be sit idly at the table scrawling caricature of barchester notable then yawn then turn over a book or two and evidently at a loss how kill some time without much labour
you havenot do much bertie about get any order say his sister
orders say he who on earth be there at barchester to give some order
who among the people here could possibly think it worth his while to have his head do into marble then you mean to give up your profession say she
no i donot say he go on with some absurd portrait of the bishop
look at that lotte benot it the little man all over apron and all
iwould go on with my profession at once as you call it if the governor would set i up with a studio in london but as to sculpture at barchester--i suppose half the people here donot know what a torso mean the governor will not give you a shilling to start you in london say lotte
indeed he cannot give you what would be sufficient for he have not get it
but you might start yourself very well if you please how the deuce be i to do it say he
to tell you the truth bertie youwill never make a penny by any profession thatbe what i often think myself say he not in the least offend
some man have a great gift of make money but they cannot spend it
other cannot put two shilling together but they have a great talent for all sort of outlay
i begin to think that my genius be wholly in the latter line how do you mean to live then ask the sister
i suppose i must regard myself as a young raven and look for heavenly manna besides we have all get something when the governor go yes--you ll have enough to supply yourself with glove and boot that be if the jews have not get the possession of it all
i believe they have the most of it already
i wonder bertie at your indifference that you with your talent and personal advantage should never try to settle yourself in life
i look forward with dread to the time when the governor must go
mother and madeline and i we shall be poor enough but you will have absolutely nothing sufficient for the day be the evil thereof say bertie

cela depend say the brother
will you marry a wife with money at any rate say he i willnot marry one without wife with money a nt so easy to get now-a-day the parson pick they all up and a parson will pick up the wife i mean for you if you do not look quickly about it the wife i mean be mrs bold whew-w-w-w whistle bertie a widow she be very beautiful say charlotte
with a son and heir already to my hand say bertie
a baby that will very likely die say charlotte
i donot see that say bertie

only mark this the money must be sure and the income at my own disposal at any rate for the lady s life charlotte be explain to her brother that he must make love for himself if he mean to carry on the matter and be encourage he to so by warm eulogium on eleanor s beauty when the signora be bring into the drawing-room
when at home and subject to the gaze of none but her own family she allow herself to be drag about by two person and her two bearer now deposit she on the sofa
she be not quite so grand in her apparel as she have be at the bishop s party but yet she be dress with much care and though there be a look of care and pain about her eye she be even by daylight extremely beautiful
well madeline so ibe go to be marry bertie begin as soon as the servant have withdraw
therebe no other foolish thing leave that you havenot do say madeline and therefore you be quite right to try that oh you think itbe a foolish thing do you say he
therebe lotte advise i to marry by all mean

come mad i be only joke you know that

she want i to marry mrs bold
shebe a widow with lot of tin a fine baby a beautiful complexion and the george and dragon hotel up in high street

instead of be vapid say lotte i call she a very lovely woman
she be by far the lovely woman in the room the other night that be except you madeline even the compliment do not soften the asperity of the maim beauty

the death of twenty husband should not make i undergo such a penance
it be as much a relic of paganism as the sacrifice of a hindu woman at the burning of her husband s body
if not so bloody it be quite as barbarous and quite as useless but you donot blame she for that say bertie
she do it because itbe the custom of the country

she be just one of those english nonentity who would tie her head up in a bag for three month every summer if her mother and her grandmother have tie up their head before she

what you mean be that itbe very hard for a fool not to be a fool say madeline
bertie stanhope have so much knock about the world from his early year that he have not retain much respect for the gravity of english custom but even to his mind an idea present itself that perhaps in a wife true british prejudice would not in the long run be less agreeable than anglo-italian freedom from restraint
he do not exactly say so but he express the idea in another way
i fancy say he that if i be to die and then walk i should think that my widow look well in one of those cap than any other kind of head-dress yes--and youwould fancy also that she could do nothing well than shut herself up and cry for you or else burn herself
but she would think differently
shewould probably wear one of those horrid she-helmet because shewould want the courage not to do so but shewould wear it with a heart longing for the time when she might be allow to throw it off
i hate such shallow false pretence
for my part i would let the world say what it please and show no grief if i feel none and perhaps not if i do but wear a widow s cap willnot lessen her fortune say charlotte
or increase it say madeline
then why on earth do she do it but lotte s object be to make she put it off say bertie
if it be true that she have get twelve hundred a year quite at her own disposal and she be not utterly vulgar in her manner i would advise you to marry she

and oh charlotte say the other
you donot mean to say that no man can love a woman unless he be a fool i mean very much the same thing that any man who be willing to sacrifice his interest to get possession of a pretty face be a fool
pretty face be to be have cheap than that
i hate your mawkish sentimentality lotte
you know as well as i do in what way husband and wife generally live together you know how far the warmth of conjugal affection can withstand the trial of a bad dinner of a rainy day or of the least privation which poverty bring with it you know what freedom a man claim for himself what slavery he would exact from his wife if he could
and you know also how wife generally obey
marriage mean tyranny on one side and deceit on the other
i say that a man be a fool to sacrifice his interest for such a bargain
a woman too generally have no other way of living but bertie have no other way of living say charlotte
then in god s name let he marry mrs bold say madeline
and so it be settle between they
but let the gentle-hearted reader be under no apprehension whatsoever
it be not destine that eleanor shall marry mr slope or bertie stanhope
and here perhaps it may be allow to the novelist to explain his view on a very important point in the art of tell tale
he venture to reprobate that system which go so far to violate all proper confidence between the author and his reader by maintain nearly to the end of the third volume a mystery as to the fate of their favourite personage
nay more and bad than this be too frequently do
have not often the profound effort of genius be use to baffle the aspiration of the reader to raise false hope and false fear and to give rise to expectation which be never realise
be not promise all but make of delightful horror in lieu of which the writer produce nothing but commonplace reality in his final chapter
and be there not a species of deceit in this to which the honesty of the present age should lend no countenance
and what can be the worth of that solicitude which a peep into the third volume can utterly dissipate
what the value of those literary charm which be absolutely destroy by their enjoyment
when we have once learn what be the picture before which be hung mrs radcliffe s solemn curtain we feel no further interest about either the frame or the veil
they be to we merely a receptacle for old bone and inappropriate coffin which we would wish to have decently bury out of our sight
and then how grievous a thing it be to have the pleasure of your novel destroy by the ill-consider triumph of a previous reader
oh you neednot be alarm for augusta of course she accept gustavus in the end how very ill-natured you be susan say kitty with tear in her eye i donot care a bit about it now dear kitty if you will read my book you may defy the ill-nature of your sister
there shall be no secret that she can tell you
nay take the last chapter if you please--learn from its page all the result of our troubled story and the story shall have lose none of its interest if indeed there be any interest in it to lose
our doctrine be that the author and the reader should move along together in full confidence with each other
let the personage of the drama undergo ever so completely a comedy of error among themselves but let the spectator never mistake the syracusan for the ephesian otherwise he be one of the dupe and the part of a dupe be never dignified
i would not for the value of this chapter have it believe by a single reader that my eleanor could bring herself to marry mr slope or that she should be sacrifice to a bertie stanhope
but among the good folk of barchester many believe both the one and the other

there be a quiet enduring grateful sweetness about her face which grow so strongly upon those who know she as to make the great praise of her beauty which come from her old friend appear marvellously exaggerated to those who be only slightly acquainted with she
her loveliness be like that of many landscape which require to be often see to be fully enjoy
there be a depth of dark clear brightness in her eye which be lose upon a quick observer a character about her mouth which only show itself to those with whom she familiarly converse a glorious form of head the perfect symmetry of which require the eye of an artist for its appreciation
she have none of that dazzle brilliancy of that voluptuous rubens beauty of that pearly whiteness and those vermilion tint which immediately entrance with the power of a basilisk man who come within reach of madeline neroni
it be all be impossible to resist the signora but no one be call upon for any resistance towards eleanor
you might begin to talk to she as though she be your sister and it would not be till your head be on your pillow that the truth and intensity of her beauty would flash upon you that the sweetness of her voice would come upon your ear

eleanor hurry out of the room to re-adjust her cap mutter some unnecessary apology about her baby
and while she be go we will briefly go back and state what have be hitherto the result of mr slope s meditation on his scheme of matrimony
his inquiry as to the widow s income have at any rate be so far successful as to induce he to determine to go on with the speculation
as regard mr harding he have also resolve to do what he could without injury to himself

his object be to instigate a little rebellion on the part of the bishop
he think that such a state of thing would be advisable not only in respect to messrs harding and quiverful but also in the affair of the diocese generally
mr slope be by no mean of the opinion that dr proudie be fit to rule but he conscientiously think it wrong that his brother clergy should be subject to petticoat government
he therefore make up his mind to infuse a little of his spirit into the bishop sufficient to induce he to oppose his wife though not enough to make he altogether insubordinate
he have therefore take the opportunity of again speak to his lordship about the hospital and have endeavour to make it appear that after all it would be unwise to exclude mr harding from the appointment
mr slope however have a hard task than he have imagine
mrs proudie anxious to assume to herself as much as possible of the merit of patronage have write to mrs quiverful request she to call at the palace and have then explain to that matron with much mystery condescension and dignity the good that be in store for she and her progeny

such incense as this be not unpleasing to mrs proudie and she make the most of it

chapter xix barchester by moonlight there be much cause for grief and occasional perturbation of spirit in the stanhope family but yet they rarely seem to be grieve or to be disturb
it be the peculiar gift of each of they that each be able to bear his or her own burden without complaint and perhaps without sympathy
they habitually look on the sunny side of the wall if there be a gleam on the either side for they to look at and if there be none they endure the shade with an indifference which if not stoical answer the end at which the stoics aim
old stanhope could not but feel that he have ill-perform his duty as a father and a clergyman and could hardly look forward to his own death without grief at the position in which he would leave his family
his income for many year have be as high as l 3000 a year and yet they have among they no other provision than their mother s fortune of l 10 000
he have not only spend his income but be in debt
yet with all this he seldom show much outward sign of trouble
it be the same with the mother
if she add little to the pleasure of her child she detract still less she neither grumble at her lot nor speak much of her past or future suffering as long as she have a maid to adjust her dress and have those dress well make nature with she be satisfied
it be the same with her child
charlotte never rebuke her father with the prospect of their future poverty nor do it seem to grieve she that she be become an old maid so quickly her temper be rarely ruffle and if we might judge by her appearance she be always happy
the signora be not so sweet-temper but she possess much enduring courage she seldom complain--never indeed to her family
though she have a cause for affliction which would have utterly break down the heart of most woman as beautiful as she and as devoid of all religious support yet she bear her suffering in silence or allude to it only to elicit the sympathy and stimulate the admiration of the man with whom she flirt
as to bertie one would have imagine from the sound of his voice and the gleam of his eye that he have not a sorrow nor a care in the world
nor have he
he be incapable of anticipate tomorrow s grief
the prospect of future want no more disturb his appetite than do that of the butcher s knife disturb the appetite of the sheep
such be the usual tenor of their way but there be rare exception
occasionally the father would allow an angry glance to fall from his eye and the lion would send forth a low dangerous roar as though he meditate some deed of blood
occasionally also madame neroni would become bitter against mankind more than usually antagonistic to the world s decency and would seem as though she be about to break from her mooring and allow herself to be carry forth by the tide of her feeling to utter ruin and shipwreck
she however like the rest of they have no real feeling could feel no true passion
in that be her security
before she resolve on any contemplated escapade she would make a small calculation and generally sum up that the stanhope villa or even barchester close be well than the world at large
they be most irregular in their hour
the father be generally the early in the breakfast-parlour and charlotte would soon follow and give he coffee but the other breakfast anywhere anyhow and at any time
on the morning after the archdeacon s futile visit to the palace dr stanhope come down stair with an ominously dark look about his eyebrow his white lock be rougher than usual and he breathe thickly and loudly as he take his seat in his arm-chair

be you a whewellite or a brewsterite or a t othermanite mrs bold say charlotte who know a little about everything and have read about a third of each of the book to which she allude

they have thus walk round the bishop s ground through which the river run and round the cathedral and adjacent field and it be past eleven before they reach the doctor s door

she look somewhat sad and melancholy but not more so perhaps than be sufficient to excite additional interest in the bosom of mr slope and she be soon deep in whisper intercourse with that happy gentleman who be allow to find a resting-place on her sofa
the signora have a way of whispering that be peculiarly her own and be exactly the reverse of that which prevail among great tragedian
the great tragedian hiss out a positive whisper make with bated breath and produce by inarticulate tongue-form sound but yet he be audible through the whole house
the signora however use no hiss and produce all her word in a clear silver tone but they could only be hear by the ear into which they be pour
