chapter xx mr arabin the reverend francis arabin fellow of lazarus late professor of poetry at oxford and present vicar of st ewold in the diocese of barchester must now be introduce personally to the reader
and as he will fill a conspicuous place in this volume it be desirable that he should be make to stand before the reader s eye by the aid of such portraiture as the author be able to produce
it be to be regret that no mental method of daguerreotype or photography have yet be discover by which the character of man can be reduce to write and put into grammatical language with an unerring precision of truthful description
how often do the novelist feel ay and the historian also and the biographer that he have conceive within his mind and accurately depict on the tablet of his brain the full character and personage of a man and that nevertheless when he fly to pen and ink to perpetuate the portrait his word forsake elude disappoint and play the deuce with he till at the end of a dozen page the man describe have no more resemblance to the man conceive than the sign board at the corner of the street have to the duke of cambridge
and yet such mechanical descriptive skill would hardly give more satisfaction to the reader than the skill of the photographer do to the anxious mother desirous to possess an absolute duplicate of her beloved child
the likeness be indeed true but it be a dull dead unfeele inauspicious likeness
the face be indeed there and those look at it will know at once whose image it be but the owner of the face will not be proud of the resemblance
there be no royal road to learn no short cut to the acquirement of any art
let photographer and daguerreotyper do what they will and improve as they may with further skill on that which skill have already do they will never achieve a portrait of the human face as we may under the burden which we so often feel too heavy for our shoulder we must either bear they up like man or own ourselves too weak for the work we have undertake
there be no way of write well and also of write easily
labor omnia vincit improbus
such should be the choose motto of every labourer and it may be that labour if adequately enduring may suffice at last to produce even some not untrue resemblance of the rev
francis arabin
of his doing in the world and of the sort of fame which he have achieve enough have already be say
it have also be say that he be forty year of age and still unmarried
he be the young son of a country gentleman of small fortune in the north of england
at an early age he go to winchester and be intend by his father for new college but though studious as a boy he be not studious within the prescribed limit and at the age of eighteen he leave school with a character for talent but without a scholarship
all that he have obtain over and above the advantage of his character be a gold medal for english verse and hence be derive a strong presumption on the part of his friend that he be destine to add another name to the imperishable list of english poet
from winchester he go to oxford and be enter as a commoner at balliol
here his special career very soon commence
he utterly eschew the society of fast man give no wine party keep no horse row no boat join no row and be the pride of his college tutor
such at least be his career till he have take his little go and then he commence a course of action which though not less creditable to himself as a man be hardly so much to the taste of his tutor
he become a member of a vigorous debating society and render himself remarkable there for humorous energy
though always in earnest yet his earnestness be always droll
to be true in his idea unanswerable in his syllogism and just in his aspiration be not enough for he
he have fail fail in his own opinion as well as that of other when other come to know he if he could not reduce the argument of his opponent to an absurdity and conquer both by wit and reason
to say that his object be ever to raise a laugh would be most untrue
he hat such common and unnecessary evidence of satisfaction on the part of his hearer
a joke that require to be laugh at be with he not worth utter
he could appreciate by a keener sense than that of his ear the success of his wit and would see in the eye of his auditory whether or no he be understand and appreciate
he have be a religious lad before he leave school
that is he have addict himself to a party of religion and have do so have receive that benefit which most man do who become partisan in such a cause
we be much too apt to look at schism in our church as an unmitigated evil
moderate schism if there may be such a thing at any rate call attention to the subject draw its supporter who would otherwise have be inattentive to the matter and teach man to think about religion
how great an amount of good of this description have follow that movement of the church of england which commence with the publication of froude s remain
as a boy young arabin take up the cudgel on the side of the tractarians and at oxford he sit for a while at the foot of the great newman
to this cause he lend all his faculty
for it he concoct verse for it he make speech for it he scintillate the bright spark of his quiet wit
for it he eat and drank and dress and have his being
in due process of time he take his degree and write himself b a but he do not do so with any remarkable amount of academical eclat
he have occupy himself too much with high church matter and the polemic politic and outward demonstration usually concurrent with high churchmanship to devote himself with sufficient vigour to the acquisition of a double first
he be not a double first nor even a first class man but he revenge himself on the university by put first and double first out of fashion for the year and laugh down a specie of pedantry which at the age of twenty-three leave no room in a man s mind for graver subject than conic section or greek accent
greek accent however and conic section be esteem necessary at balliol and there be no admittance there for mr arabin within the list of its fellow
lazarus however the rich and the most comfortable abode of oxford don open its bosom to the young champion of a church militant
mr arabin be ordain and become a fellow soon after take his degree and shortly after that be choose professor of poetry
and now come the moment of his great danger
after many mental struggle and an agony of doubt which may be well surmise the great prophet of the tractarians confess himself a roman catholic
mr newman leave the church of england and with he carry many a waverer
he do not carry off mr arabin but the escape which that gentleman have be a very narrow one
he leave oxford for a while that he might meditate in complete peace on the step which appear for he to be all but unavoidable and shut himself up in a little village on the sea-shore of one of our remotest county that he might learn by commune with his own soul whether or no he could with a safe conscience remain within the pale of his mother church
thing would have go badly with he there have he be leave entirely to himself
every thing be against he all his worldly interest require he to remain a protestant and he look on his worldly interest as a legion of foe to get the well of whom be a point of extreme honour
in his then state of ecstatic agony such a conquest would have cost he little but it cost he much to get over the idea of choose the church of england he should be open in his own mind to the charge that he have be lead to such a choice by unworthy motif
then his heart be against he he love with a strong and eager love the man who have hitherto be his guide and yearn to follow his footstep
his taste be against he the ceremony and pomp of the church of rome their august feast and solemn fast invite his imagination and please his eye
his flesh be against he how great an aid would it be to a poor weak waver man to be constrain to high moral duty self-denial obedience and chastity by law which be certain in their enactment and not to be break without loud palpable unmistakable sin
then his faith be against he he require to believe so much pant so early to give sign of his belief deem it so insufficient to wash himself simply in the water of jordan that some great deed such as that of forsake everything for a true church have for he allurement almost past withstand
mr arabin be at this time a very young man and when he leave oxford for his far retreat be much too confident in his power of fence and too apt to look down on the ordinary sense of ordinary people to expect aid in the battle that he have to fight from any chance inhabitant on the spot which he have select
but providence be good to he and there in that all but desolate place on the storm-beat shore of that distant sea he meet one who gradually change his mind quiet his imagination and teach he something of a christian s duty
when mr arabin leave oxford he be inclined to look upon the rural clergyman of most english parish almost with contempt
it be his ambition should he remain within the fold of the church to do somewhat towards redeem and rectify their inferiority and to assist in infuse energy and faith into the heart of christian minister who be as he think too often satisfied to go through life without much show of either
and yet it be from such a one that mr arabin in his extreme need receive that aid which he so much require
it be from a poor curate of a small cornish parish that he first learn to know that the high law for the governance of a christian s duty must act from within and not from without that no man can become a serviceable servant solely by obedience to write edict and that the safety which he be about to seek within the gate of rome be no other than the selfish freedom from personal danger which the bad soldier attempt to gain who counterfeit illness on the eve of battle
mr arabin return to oxford a humbler but a well and a happy man and from that time forth he put his shoulder to the wheel as a clergyman of the church for which he have be educate
the intercourse of those among whom he familiarly live keep he staunch to the principle of that system of the church to which he have always belong
since his severance from mr newman no one have have so strong an influence over he as the head of his college
during the time of his expected apostasy dr gwynne have not feel much predisposition in favour of the young fellow
though a high churchman himself within moderate limit dr gwynne feel no sympathy with man who could not satisfy their faith with the thirty-nine articles
he regard the enthusiasm of such as newman as a state of mind more nearly ally to madness than to religion and when he see it evince by a very young man be incline to attribute a good deal of it to vanity
dr gwynne himself though a religious man be also a thoroughly practical man of the world and he regard with no favourable eye the tenet of any one who look on the two thing as incompatible
when he find mr arabin be a half roman he begin to regret all that he do towards bestow a fellowship on so unworthy a recipient and when again he learn that mr arabin would probably complete his journey to rome he regard with some satisfaction the fact that in such case the fellowship would be again vacant
when however mr arabin return and profess himself a confirm protestant the master of lazarus again open his arm to he and gradually he become the pet of the college
for some little time he be saturnine silent and unwilling to take any prominent part in university broil but gradually his mind recover or rather make its tone and he become know as a man always ready at a moment s notice to take up the cudgel in opposition to anything which savour of an evangelical bearing
he be great in sermon great on platform great at after dinner conversation and always pleasant as well as great
he take delight in election serve on committee oppose tooth and nail all project of university reform and talk jovially over his glass of port of the ruin to be commit by the whig
the ordeal through which he have go in resist the blandishment of the lady of rome have certainly do much towards the strengthening of his character
although in small and outward matter he be self-confident enough nevertheless in thing affect the inner man he aim at a humility of spirit which would never have be attractive to he but for that visit to the coast of cornwall
this visit he now repeat every year
such be an interior view of mr arabin at the time when he accept the living of st ewold
exteriorly he be not a remarkable person
he be above the middle height well make and very active
his hair which have be jet black be now ting with gray but his face bear no sign of year
it would perhaps be wrong to say that he be handsome but his face be nevertheless high for beauty and the formation of the forehead too massive and heavy but his eye nose and mouth be perfect
there be a continual play of lambent fire about his eye which give promise of either pathos or humour whenever he essay to speak and that promise be rarely break
there be a gentle play about his mouth which declare that his wit never descend to sarcasm and that there be no ill-nature in his repartee
mr arabin be a popular man among woman but more so as a general than a special favourite
live as a fellow at oxford marriage with he have be out of the question and it may be doubt whether he have ever allow his heart to be touch
though belong to a church in which celibacy be not the required lot of its minister he have come to regard himself as one of those clergyman to whom to be a bachelor be almost a necessity
he have never look for parochial duty and his career at oxford be utterly incompatible with such domestic joy as a wife and nursery
he look on woman therefore in the same light that one see then regard by many romish priest
he like to have near he that which be pretty and amusing but woman generally be little more to he than child
he talk to they without put out all his power and listen to they without any idea that what he should hear from they could either actuate his conduct or influence his opinion
such be mr arabin the new vicar of st ewold who be go to stay with the grantlys at plumstead episcopi
mr arabin reach plumstead the day before mr harding and eleanor and the grantly family be thus enable to make his acquaintance and discuss his qualification before the arrival of the other guest
griselda be surprised to find that he look so young but she tell florinda her young sister when they have retire for the night that he do not talk at all like a young man and she decide with the authority that seventeen have over sixteen that he be not at all nice although his eye be lovely
as usual sixteen implicitly accede to the dictum of seventeen in such a matter and say that he certainly be not nice
they then branch off on the relative merit of other clerical bachelor in the vicinity and both determine without any feeling of jealousy between they that a certain rev
augustus green be by many degree the most estimable of the lot
the gentleman in question have certainly much in his favour as have a comfortable allowance from his father he could devote the whole proceed of his curacy to violet glove and unexceptionable neck tie
have thus fixedly resolve that the new comer have nothing about he to shake the pre-eminence of the exalt green the two girl go to sleep in each other s arm content with themselves and the world
mrs grantly at first sight come to much the same conclusion about her husband s favourite as her daughter have do though in seek to measure his relative value she do not compare he to mr green indeed she make no comparison by name between he and any one else but she remark to her husband that one person s swan be very often another person s geese thereby clearly show that mr arabin have not yet prove his qualification in swanhood to her satisfaction
well susan say he rather offended at hear his friend speak of so disrespectfully if you take mr arabin for a goose i cannot say that i think very highly of your discrimination a goose
no of course hebe not a goose
ihave no doubt hebe a very clever man

all i mean be that have pass one evening with he i donot find he to be absolutely a paragon

in truth they be both right
mr arabin be a diffident man in social intercourse with those whom he do not intimately know when place in situation which it be his business to fill and discuss matter with which it be his duty to be conversant mr arabin be from habit braze-face enough
when stand on a platform in exeter hall no man would be less mazed than he by the eye of the crowd before he for such be the work which his profession have call on he to perform but he shrink from a strong expression of opinion in general society and his do so not uncommonly make it appear that he consider the company not worth the trouble of his energy
he be averse to dictate when the place do not seem to he to justify dictation and as those subject on which people wish to hear he speak be such as he be accustom to treat with decision he generally shun the trap there be lay to allure he into discussion and by do so not unfrequently subject himself to such charge as those bring against he by mrs grantly
mr arabin as he sit at his open window enjoy the delicious moonlight and gaze at the gray tower of the church which stand almost within the rectory ground little dream that he be the subject of so many friendly or unfriendly criticism
consider how much we be all give to discuss the character of other and discuss they often not in the strict spirit of charity it be singular how little we be inclined to think that other can speak ill-naturedly of we and how angry and hurt we be when proof reach we that they have do so
it be hardly too much to say that we all of we occasionally speak of our dear friend in a manner which those dear friend would very little like to hear themselves mention and that we nevertheless expect that our dear friend shall invariably speak of we as though they be blind to all our fault but keenly alive to every shade of our virtue
it do not occur to mr arabin that he be speak of at all
it seem to he when he compare himself with his host that he be a person of so little consequence to any that he be worth no one s word or thought
he be utterly alone in the world as regard domestic tie and those inner familiar relation which be hardly possible between other than husband and wife parent and child or brother and sister
he have often discuss with himself the necessity of such bond for a man s happiness in this world and have generally satisfy himself with the answer that happiness in this world be not a necessity
herein he deceive himself or rather try to do so
he like other yearn for the enjoyment of whatever he see enjoyable and though he attempt with the modern stoicism of so many christians to make himself believe that joy and sorrow be matter which here should be hold as perfectly indifferent those thing be not indifferent to he
he be tired of his oxford room and his college life
he regard the wife and child of his friend with something like envy he all but covet the pleasant drawing-room with its pretty window opening on to lawn and flower-bed the apparel of the comfortable house and--above all--the air of home which encompass all
it will be say that no time can have be fit for such desire on his part as this of a living among field and garden of a house which a wife would grace
it be true there be a difference between the opulence of plumstead and the modest economy of st ewold but surely mr arabin be not a man to sigh after wealth
of all man his friend would have unanimously declare he be the last to do so
but how little our friend know we
in his period of stoical rejection of this world s happiness he have cast from he as utter dross all anxiety as to fortune
he have as it be proclaim himself to be indifferent to promotion and those who chiefly admire his talent and would mainly have exert to secure they their deserved reward have take he at his word
and now if the truth must out he feel himself disappointed--disappoint not by they but by himself
the daydream of his youth be over and at the age of forty he feel that he be not fit to work in the spirit of an apostle
he have mistake himself and learn his mistake when it be past remedy
he have profess himself indifferent to mitre and diaconal residence to rich living and pleasant glebe and now he have to own to himself that he be sigh for the good thing of other man on whom in his pride he have venture to look down
not for wealth in its vulgar sense have he ever sigh not for the enjoyment of rich thing have he ever long but for the allotted share of worldly bliss which a wife and child and happy home could give he for that usual amount of comfort which he have venture to reject as unnecessary for he he do now feel that he would have be wise to search
he know that his talent his position and his friend would have win for he promotion have he put himself in the way of win it
instead of do so he have allow himself an income of some l 300 a year should he by marry throw up his fellowship
such at the age of forty be the worldly result of labour which the world have choose to regard as successful
the world also think that mr arabin be in his own estimation sufficiently pay

the world be mistaken and mr arabin be begin to ascertain that such be the case
and here may i beg the reader not to be hard in the judgement upon this man
be not the state at which he have arrive the natural result of effort to reach that which be not the condition of humanity
be not modern stoicism build though it be on christianity as great an outrage on human nature as be the stoicism of the ancient
the philosophy of zeno be build on true law but on true law misunderstood and therefore misapply
it be the same with our stoic here who would teach we that wealth and worldly comfort and happiness on earth be not worth the search
also for a doctrine which can find no believe pupil and no true teacher
the case of mr arabin be the more singular as he belong to a branch of the church of england well inclined to regard its temporality with avow favour and have habitually live with man who be accustom to much worldly comfort
but such be his idiosyncrasy that these very fact have produce within he in early life a state of mind that be not natural to he
he be content to be a high churchman if he could be so on principle of his own and could strike out a course show a marked difference from those with whom he consort
he be ready to be a partisan as long as he be allow to have a course of action and of thought unlike that of his party
his party have indulge he and he begin to feel that his party be right and himself wrong but when such a conviction be too late to be of service to he
he discover when much be discovery be no long serviceable that it would have be worth his while to have work for the usual pay assign to work in this world and have earn a wife and child with a carriage for they to sit in to have earn a pleasant dining-room in which his friend could drink his wine and the power of walk up in the high street of his country town with the knowledge that all its tradesman would have gladly welcome he within their door
other man arrive at those conviction in their start of life and so work up to they
to he they have come when they be too late to be of use
it have be say that mr arabin be a man of pleasantry and it may be think that such a state of mind as that describe would be antagonistic to humour
but surely such be not the case
wit be the outward mental casing of the man and have no more to do with the inner mind of thought and feeling than have the rich brocade garment of the priest at the altar with the asceticism of the anchorite below they whose skin be torment with sackcloth and whose body be half flay with rod
nay will not such a one often rejoice more than any other in the rich show of outer apparel
will it not be food for his pride to feel that he groan inwardly while he shin outwardly
so it be with the mental effort which man make
those which they show forth daily to the world be often the opposite of the inner working of the spirit
in the archdeacon s drawing-room mr arabin have sparkle with his usual unaffected brilliancy but when he retire to his bed-room he sit there sad at his open window repine within himself that he also have no wife no bairn no soft award of lawn duly mow for he to be on no herd of attendant curate no bowing from the banker s clerk no rich rectory
that apostleship that he have think of have evade his grasp and he be now only vicar of st ewold s with a taste for a mitre
truly he have fall between two stool
chapter xxi st ewold s parsonage when mr harding and mrs bold reach the rectory on the following morning the archdeacon and his friend be at st ewold s

yes say he join she

the pity be that we cannot do the one without the other
but be we not here to fight
be not ours a church militant
what be all our work but fighting and hard fighting if it be well do but not with each other thatbe as it may be
the same complaint which you make of i for battle with another clergyman of our own church the mahometan would make against i for battle with the error of a priest of rome
yet surely you would not be incline to say that i should be wrong to do battle with such as he
a pagan too with his multiplicity of god would think it equally odd that the christian and the mahometan should disagree ah
but you wage your war about trifle so bitterly war about trifle say he be always bitter especially among neighbour
when the difference be great and the party comparative stranger man quarrel with courtesy
what combatant be ever so eager as two brother but do not such contention bring scandal on the church more scandal would fall on the church if there be no such contention
we have but one way to avoid they--that of acknowledge a common head of our church whose word on all point of doctrine shall be authoritative
such a termination of our difficulty be alluring enough
it have charm which be irresistible to many and all but irresistible i own to i you speak now of the church of rome say eleanor
no say he not necessarily the church of rome but of a church with a head
have it please god to vouchsafe to we such a church our path would have be easy
but easy path have not be think good for we he pause and stand silent for a while think of the time when he have so nearly sacrifice all he have his power of mind his free agency the fresh running water of his mind s fountain his very inner self for an easy path in which no fighting would be need and then he continue what you say be partly true our contention do bring on we some scandal
the outer world though it constantly revile we for our human infirmity and throw in our tooth the fact that be clergyman we be still no more then man demand of we that we should do our work with godlike perfection
there be nothing godlike about we we differ from each other with the acerbity common to man--we allow difference on subject of divine origin to produce among we antipathy and enmity which be anything but divine
this be all true
but what would you have in place of it
there be no infallible head for a church on earth
this dream of believe man have be try and we see in italy and in spain what have become of it
grant that there be and have be no bickering within the pale of the pope s church
such an assumption would be utterly untrue but let we grant it and then let we say which church have incur the heavy scandal there be a quiet earnestness about mr arabin as he half acknowledge and half defend himself from the charge bring against he which surprise eleanor
she have be use all her life to listen to clerical discussion but the point at issue between the disputant have so seldom be of more than temporal significance as to have leave on her mind no feeling of reverence for such subject
there have always be a hard worldly leaven of the love either of income or power in the strain that she have hear there have be no panting for the truth no aspiration after religious purity
it have always be take for grant by those around she that they be indubitably right that there be no ground for doubt that the hard uphill work of ascertain what the duty of a clergyman should be have be already accomplish in full and that what remain for an active militant parson to do be to hold his own against all comer

it be so easy to condemn say he continue the thread of his thought
i know no life that must be so delicious as that of a writer for newspaper or a lead member of the opposition--to thunder forth accusation against man in power show up the bad side of every thing that be produce to pick hole in every coat to be indignant sarcastic jocose moral or supercilious to damn with faint praise or crush with open calumny
what can be so easy as this when the critic have to be responsible for nothing
you condemn what i do but put yourself in my position and do the reverse and then see if i cannot condemn you oh
mr arabin i do not condemn you pardon i you do mrs bold--you as one of the world you be now the opposition member you be now compose your lead article and well and bitterly you do it

it will however give you no trouble to write another article next week in which we or some of we shall be twit with an unseemly apathy in matter of our vocation

barely sixteen you may call it a square it would do very well for a round table suggest the ex-warden
now there be something peculiarly unorthodox in the archdeacon s estimation in the idea of a round table
he have always be accustomed to a goodly board of decent length comfortably elongate itself accord to the number of guest nearly black with perpetual rubbing and as bright as a mirror
now round dinner table be generally of oak or else of such new construction as not to have acquire the peculiar hue which be so pleasing to he
he connect they with what he call the nasty new fangle method of leave cloth on the table as though to warn people that they be not to sit long
in his eye there be something democratic and parvenu in a round table
he imagine that dissenter and calico-printer chiefly use they and perhaps a few literary lion more conspicuous for their wit than their gentility
he be a little flurry at the idea of such an article be introduce into the diocese by a protege of his own and at the instigation of his father-in-law
a round dinner-table say he with some heat be the most abominable article of furniture that ever be invent
i hope that arabin have more taste than to allow such a thing in his house poor mr harding feel himself completely snub and of course say nothing far but mr arabin who have yield submissively in the small matter of the cellar and kitchen grate find himself oblige to oppose reform which might be of a nature too expensive for his pocket
but it seem to i archdeacon that i cannot very well lengthen the room without pull down the wall and if i pull down the wall i must build it up again then if i throw out a bow on this side i must do the same on the other then if i do it for the ground floor i must carry it up to the floor above
that will be put a new front to the house and will cost i suppose a couple of hundred pound
the ecclesiastical commissioner will hardly assist i when they hear that my grievance consist in have a dining-room only sixteen foot long the archdeacon proceed to explain that nothing would be easy than add six foot to the front of the dining-room without touch any other of the house

mr arabin however be firm and although the archdeacon fuss and fume about it would not give way

but say mrs grantly what if the priestess insist on have both the room enlarge the priestess in that case must do it for herself mrs grantly i have no doubt she will be well able to do so reply the lady to do that and many more wonderful thing
i be quite sure that the priestess of st ewold when she do come willnot come empty-handed mr arabin however do not appear well inclined to enter into speculative expense on such a chance as this and therefore any material alteration in the house the cost of which could not fairly be make to lie at the door either of the ecclesiastical commission or of the estate of the late incumbent be taboo
with this essential exception the archdeacon order suggest and carry all point before he in a manner very much to his own satisfaction
a close observer have there be one there might have see that his wife have be quite as useful in the matter as himself
no one know well than mrs grantly the appurtenance necessary to a comfortable house
she do not however think it necessary to lay claim to any of the glory which her lord and master be so ready to appropriate as his own
have go through their work effectively and systematically the party return to plumstead well satisfied with their expedition
chapter xxii the thornes of ullathorne on the following sunday mr arabin be to read himself in at his new church
it be agree at the rectory that the archdeacon should go over with he and assist at the reading-desk and that mr harding should take the archdeacon s duty at plumstead church
mrs grantly have her school and her bun to attend to and profess that she could not be spar but mrs bold be to accompany they
it be far agree also that they would lunch at the squire s house and return home after the afternoon service
wilfred thorne esq of ullathorne be the squire of st ewold s or rather the squire of ullathorne for the domain of the modern landlord be of wide notoriety than the fame of the ancient saint
he be a fair speciman of what that race have come to in our day which a century ago be as we be tell fairly represent by squire western
if that representation be a true one few class of man can have make fast stride in improvement
mr thorne however be a man possess of quite a sufficient number of foible to lay he open to much ridicule
he be still a bachelor be about fifty and be not a little proud of his person
when live at home at ullathorne there be not much room for such pride and there therefore he always look like a gentleman and like that which he certainly be the first man in his parish
but during the month or six week which he annually spend in london he try so hard to look like a great man there also which he certainly be not that he be put down as a fool by many at his club
he be a man of considerable literary attainment in a certain way and on certain subject
his favourite author be montaigne and burton and he know more perhaps than any other man in his own county and the next to it of the english essayist of the two last century
he possess complete set of the idler the spectator the tatler the guardian and the rambler and would discourse by hour together on the superiority of such publication to anything which have since be produce in our edinburghs and quarterlies
he be a great proficient in all question of genealogy and know enough of almost every gentleman s family in england to say of what blood and lineage be descend all those who have any claim to be consider as possessor of any such luxury
for blood and lineage he himself have a must profound respect
he count back his own ancestor to some period long antecedent to the conquest and could tell you if you would listen to he how it have come to pass that they like cedric the saxon have be permit to hold their own among the norman baron
it be not accord to his showing on account of any weak complaisance on the part of his family towards their norman neighbour
some ealfried of ullathorne once fortify his own castle and hold out not only that but the then exist cathedral of barchester also against one godfrey de burgh in the time of king john and mr thorne possess the whole history of the siege write on vellum and illuminate in a most costly manner
it little signify that no one could read the writing as have that be possible no one could have understand the language
mr thorne could however give you all the particular in good english and have no objection to do so
it would be unjust to say that he look down in man whose family be of recent date
he do not do so
he frequently consort with such and have choose many of his friend from among they
but he look on they as great millionaire be apt to look on those who have small income as man who have sophocles at their finger end regard those who know nothing of greek
they might doubtless be good sort of people entitle to much praise for virtue very admirable for talent highly respectable in every way but they be without the one great good gift
such be mr thorne s way of think on this matter nothing could atone for the loss of good blood nothing could neutralise its good effect
few indeed be now possess of it but the possession be on that account the more precious
it be very pleasant to hear mr thorne descant on this matter
be you in your ignorance to surmise that such a one be of a good family because the head of his family be a baronet of an old date he would open his eye with a delightful look of affect surprise and modestly remind you that baronetcy only date from james i
he would gently sigh if you speak of the blood of the fitzgeralds and de burghs would hardly allow the claim of the howard and lowther and have before now allude to the talbot as a family who have hardly yet achieve the full honour of a pedigree
in speak once of a wide spread race whose name have receive the honour of three coronet scion from which sit for various constituency some one of whose member have be in almost every cabinet form during this present century a brilliant race such as there be few in england mr thorne call they all dirt
he have not intend any disrespect to these man
he admire they in many sens and allow they their privilege without envy
he have merely mean to express his feeling that the stream which run through their not vein be yet purify by time to that perfection have not become so genuine an ichor as to be worthy of be call blood in the genealogical sense
when mr arabin be first introduce to he mr thorne have immediately suggest that he be one of the arabins of uphill stanton
mr arabin reply that he be a very distant relative of the family allude to
to this mr thorne surmise that the relationship could not be very distant
mr arabin assure he that it be so distant that the family know nothing of each other
mr thorne laugh his gentle laugh at this and tell mr arabin that there be not exist no branch of his family separate from the parent stock at an early date than the reign of elizabeth and that therefore mr arabin could not call himself distant
mr arabin himself be quite clearly an arabin of uphill stanton
but say the vicar uphill stanton have be sell to the de greys and have be in their hand for the last fifty year and when it have be there one hundred and fifty if it unluckily remain there so long say mr thorne your descendant will not be a whit the less entitled to describe themselves as be of the family of uphill stanton
thank god no de grey can buy that--and thank god--no arabin and no thorne can sell it in politic mr thorne be an unflinche conservative
he look on those fifty-three trojans who as mr dod tell we censure free trade in november 1852 as the only patriot leave among the public man of england
when that terrible crisis of free trade have arrive when the repeal of the corn law be carry by those very man whom mr thorne have hitherto regard as the only possible saviour of his country he be for a time paralyse
his country be lose but that be comparatively a small thing
other country have flourish and fall and the human race still go on improve under god s providence
but now all trust in human faith must for ever be at an end
not only must ruin come but it must come through the apostasy of those who have be regard as the truest of true believer
politic in england as a pursuit for gentleman must be at an end
have mr thorne be tread under foot by a whig he could have bear it as a tory and a martyr but to be so utterly throw over and deceive by those he have so earnestly support so thoroughly trust be more than he could endure and live
he therefore cease to live as a politician and refuse to hold any converse with the world at large on the state of the country
such be mr thorne s impression for the first two or three year after sir robert peel s apostasy but by degree his temper as do that of other cool down
he begin once more to move about to frequent the bench and the market and to be see at dinner shoulder to shoulder with some of those who have so cruelly betray he
it be a necessity for he to live and that plan of his for avoid the world do not answer
he however have other around he who still maintain the same staunch principle of protection--man like himself who be too true to flinch at the cry of a mob--have their own way of console themselves
they be and feel themselves to be the only true depository leave of certain eleusinian mystery of certain deep and wondrous service of worship by which alone the god could be rightly approach
to they and they only be it now give to know these thing and to perpetuate they if that might still be do by the careful and secret education of their child
we have read how private and peculiar form of worship have be carry on from age to age in family which to the outer world have apparently adhere to the service of some ordinary church
and so by degree it be with mr thorne
he learn at length to listen calmly while protection be talk of as a thing dead although he know within himself that it be still quick with a mystic life
nor be he without a certain pleasure that such knowledge though give to he should be debar from the multitude
he become accustom to hear even among country gentleman that free trade be after all not so bad and to bear this without dispute although conscious within himself that everything good in england have go with his old palladium
he have within he something of the feeling of cato who glory that he could kill himself because romans be no long worthy of their name
mr thorne have no thought of kill himself be a christian and still possess his l 4000 a year but the feeling be not on that account the less comfortable
mr thorne be a sportsman and have be active though not outrageous in his sport
previous to the great downfall of politic in his country he have support the hunt by every mean in his power
he have preserve game till no goose or turkey could show a tail in the parish of st ewold s
he have plant gorse cover with more care than oak and larch
he have be more anxious for the comfort of his fox than of his ewe and lamb
no meet have be more popular than ullathorne no man s stable have be more liberally open to the horse of distant man than mr thorne s no man have say more write more or do more to keep the club up
the theory of protection could expand itself so thoroughly in the practice of the country hunt
but the great ruin come when the noble master of the barchester hound support the recreant minister in the house of lords and basely surrender his truth his manhood his friend and his honour for the hope of a garter then mr thorne give up the hunt
he do not cut his cover for that would not have be the act of a gentleman
he do not kill his fox for that accord to his light would have be murder
he do not say that his cover should not be draw or his earth stop for that would have be illegal accord to the by-law prevail among country gentleman
but he absent himself from home on the occasion of every meet at ullathorne leave the cover to their fate and could not be persuade to take his pink coat out of the press or his hunter out of his stable
this last for two year and then by degree he come round
he first appear at a neighbouring meet on a pony dress in his shooting coat as though he have trot in by accident then he walk up one morning on foot to see his favourite gorse draw and when his groom bring his mare out by chance he do not refuse to mount she
he be next persuade by one of the immortal fifty-three to bring his hunting material over to the other side of the county and take a fortnight with the hound there and so gradually he return to his old life
but in hunting as in other thing he be only support by the inward feeling of mystic superiority to those with whom he share the common breath of outer life
mr thorne do not live in solitude at ullathorne
he have a sister who be ten year old than himself and who participate in his prejudice and feeling so strongly that she be a living caricature of all his foible
she would not open a modern quarterly do not choose to see a magazine in her drawing-room and would not have pollute her finger with a shred of the times for any consideration
she speak of addison swift and steele as though they be still live regard de foe as the well know novelist of his country and thought of fielding as a young but meritorious novice in the field of romance
in poetry she be familiar with then name as late as dryden and have once be seduce into read the rape of the lock but she regard spenser as the pure type of her country s literature in this line
genealogy be her favourite insanity
those thing which be the pride of most genealogist be to her contemptible
arm and motto set she beside herself
ealfried of ullathorne have want no motto to assist he in cleave to the brisket geoffrey de burgh and ealfried s great grandfather the gigantic ullafrid have require no other arm than those which nature give he to hurl from the top of his own castle a cousin of the base invade norman
to she all modern english name be equally insignificant
hengist horsa and such like have for she the only true savour of nobility
she be not content unless she could go beyond the saxons and would certainly have christen her child have she have child by the name of the ancient britons
in some respect she be not unlike scott s ulrica and have she be give to cursing she would certainly have do so in the name of mista skogula and zernebock
not have submit to the embrace of any pollute normans as poor ulrica have do and have assist no parricide the milk of human kindness be not curdle in her bosom
she never curse therefore but bless rather
this however she do in a strange uncouth saxon manner that would have be unintelligible to any peasant but her own
as a politician miss thorne have be so thoroughly disgusted with public life by base deed long antecedent to the corn law question that that have but little move she
in her estimation her brother have be a fast young man hurry away by a too ardent temperament into democratic tendency
now happily he be bring to sounder view by see the iniquity of the world
she have not yet reconcile herself to the reform bill and still groan in spirit over the defalcation of the duke as touch the catholic emancipation
if ask whom she think the queen should take as her counsellor she would probably have name lord eldon and when remind that that venerable man be no long present in the flesh to assist we she would probably have answer with a sigh that none now could help we but the dead
in religion miss thorne be a pure druidess
we would not have it understand by that that she do actually in these latter day assist at any human sacrifice or that she be in fact hostile to the church of christ
she have adopt the christian religion as a milder form of the worship of her ancestor and always appeal to she do so as evidence that she have no prejudice against reform when it could be show that reform be salutary
this reform be the most modern of any to which she have as yet accede it be presume that british lady have give up their paint and take to some sort of petticoat before the day of st augustine
that further feminine step in advance which combine paint and petticoat together have not find votary in miss thorne
but she be a druidess in this that she regret she know not what in the usage and practice of her church
she sometimes talk and constantly think of good thing go by though she have but the faint idea of what those good thing have be
she imagine that a purity have exist which be now go that a piety have adorn our pastor and a simple docility our people for which it may be fear history give she but little true warrant
she be accustomed to speak of cranmer as though he have be the firm and most simple-minded of martyr and of elizabeth as though the pure protestant faith of her people have be the one anxiety of her life
it would have be cruel to undeceive she have it be possible but it would have be impossible to make she believe that the one be a time-serve priest willing to go any length to keep his place and that the other be in heart a papist with this sole proviso that she should be her own pope
and so miss thorne go on sigh and regretting look back to the divine right of king as the rule axiom of a golden age and cherishing low down in the bottom of her heart of heart a dear unmentione wish for the restoration of some exile stuart
who would deny she the luxury of her sigh or the sweetness of her soft regret
in her person and her dress she be perfect and well she know she own perfection
she be a small elegantly make old woman with a face from which the glow of her youth have not depart without leave some streak of a roseate hue
she be proud of her colour proud of her grey hair which she wear in short crisp curl peer out all around her face from the dainty white cap
to think of all the money that she spend in lace use to break the heart of poor mrs quiverful with her seven daughter
she be proud of her tooth which be still white and numerous proud of her bright cheery eye proud of her short jaunty step and very proud of the neat precise small foot with which those step be take
she be proud also ay very proud of the rich brocade silk in which it be her custom to ruffle through her drawing-room
we know what be the custom of the lady of branksome-- nine and twenty knight of fame hung their shield in branksome hall the lady of ullathorne be not so martial in her habit but hardly less costly
she might have boast that nine-and-twenty silken shirt might have be produce in her chamber each fit to stand alone
the nine-and-twenty shield of the scottish hero be less independent and hardly more potent to withstand any attack that might be make on they
miss thorne when fully dress might be say to have be armed cap-a-pie and she be always fully dress as far as be ever know to mortal man
for all this rich attire miss thorne be not indebted to the generosity of her brother
she have a very comfortable independence of her own which she divide among juvenile relative the milliner and the poor give much the large share to the latter
it may be imagine therefore that with all her little folly she be not unpopular
all her folly have we believe be tell
her virtue be too numerous to describe and not sufficiently interesting to deserve description
while we be on the subject of the thornes one word must be say of the house they live in
it be not a large house nor a fine house nor perhaps to modern idea a very commodious house but by those who love the peculiar colour and peculiar ornament of genuine tudor architecture it be consider a perfect gem
we beg to own ourselves among the number and therefore take this opportunity to express our surprise that so little be know by english man and woman of the beauty of english architecture
the ruin of the colosseum the campanile at florence st mark s cologne the bourse and notre dame be with our tourist as familiar as household word but they know nothing of the glory of wiltshire dorsetshire and somersetshire
nay we much question whether many noted traveller many who have pitch their tent perhaps under mount sinai be not still ignorant that there be glory in wiltshire dorsetshire and somersetshire
we beg that they will go and see
mr thorne s house be call ullathorne court and be properly so call for the house itself form two side of a quadrangle which be complete in the other two side by a wall about twenty foot high
this be build of cut stone rudely cut indeed and now much worn but of a beautiful rich tawny yellow colour the effect of that stonecrop of minute growth which it have take three century to produce
the top of this wall be ornament by huge round stone ball of the same colour as the wall itself
entrance into the court be have through a pair of iron gate so massive that no one could comfortably open or close they consequently they be rarely disturb
from the gateway two path lead obliquely across the court that to the left reach the hall-door which be in the corner make by the angle of the house and that to the right lead to the back entrance which be at the further end of the long portion of the building
with those who be now adept at contrive house accommodation it will militate much against ullathorne court that no carriage could be bring to the hall-door
if you enter ullathorne at all you must do so fair reader on foot or at least in a bath-chair
no vehicle draw by horse ever come within that iron gate
but this be nothing to the next horror that will encounter you
on enter the front door which you do by no very grand portal you find yourself immediately in the dining-room
what--no hall
exclaim my luxurious friend accustom to all the comfortable appurtenance of modern life
yes kind sir a noble hall if you will but observe it a true old english hall of excellent dimension for a country gentleman s family but if you please no dining-parlour
but mr and miss thorne be proud of this peculiarity of their dwelling though the brother be once all but tempt by his friend to alter it
they delight in the knowledge that they like cedric positively din in their true hall even though they so din tete-a-tete
but though they have never own they have feel and endeavour to remedy the discomfort of such an arrangement
a huge screen partition off the front door and a portion of the hall and from the angle so screen off a second door lead into a passage which run along the large side of the house next to the courtyard
either my reader or i must be a bad hand at topography if it be not clear that the great hall form the ground-floor of the small portion of the mansion that which be to your left as you enter the iron gate and that it occupy the whole of this wing of the building
it must be equally clear that it look out on a trim mow lawn through three quadrangular window with stone mullion each window divide into a large portion at the bottom and a small portion at the top and each portion again divide into five by perpendicular stone supporter
there may be window which give a well light than such as those and it may be as my utilitarian friend observe that the giving of light be the desire object of a window
i will not argue the point with he
indeed i cannot
but i shall not the less die in the assured conviction that no sort of description of window be capable of impart half as much happiness to mankind as that which have be adopt at ullathorne court
what--not an oriel
say miss diana de midellage
no miss diana not even an oriel beautiful as be an oriel window
it have not about it so perfect a feeling of quiet english homely comfort
let oriel window grace a college or the half public mansion of a potent peer but for the sitting room of quiet country lady of ordinary homely folk nothing can equal the square mullione window of the tudor architect
the hall be hang round with family female insipidity by lely and unprepossesse male thornes in red coat by kneller each thorne have be let into a panel in the wainscoting in the proper manner
at the further end of the room be a huge fire-place which afford much ground of difference between the brother and sister
an antiquated grate that would hold about a hundred weight of coal have be stick on the hearth by mr thorne s father
this hearth have of course be intend for the consumption of wood fagot and the iron dog for the purpose be still stand though half bury in the masonry of the grate
miss thorne be very anxious to revert to the dog
the dear good old creature be always to revert to anything and have she be systematically indulge would doubtless in time have reflect that finger be make before fork and have revert accordingly
but in the affair of the fire-place mr thorne would not revert
country gentleman around he all have comfortable grate in their dining-room
he be not exactly the man to have suggest a modern usage but he be not so far prejudiced as to banish those which his father have prepare for his use
mr thorne have indeed once suggest that with very little contrivance the front door might have be so altered as to open at least into the passage but on hear this his sister monica such be miss thorne s name have be take ill and have remain so for a week
before she come down stair she receive a pledge from her brother that the entrance should never be change in her lifetime
at the end of the hall opposite to the fire-place a door lead into the drawing-room which be of equal size and light with precisely similar window
but yet the aspect of the room be very different
it be paper and the ceiling which in the hall show the old rafter be whiten and finish with a modern cornice
miss thorne s drawing-room or as she always call it withdrawing-room be a beautiful apartment
the window open on to the full extent of the lovely trim garden immediately before the window be plot of flower in stiff stately stubborn little bed each bed surround by a stone cop of its own beyond there be a low parapet wall on which stand urn and image fawn nymph satyr and a whole tribe of pan s follower and then again beyond that a beautiful lawn slop away to a sunk fence which divide the garden from the park
mr thorne s study be at the end of the drawing-room and beyond that be the kitchen and the office
door open into both miss thorne s withdrawing-room and mr thorne s sanctum from the passage above allude to which as it come to the latter room widen itself so as to make space for the huge black oak stair which lead to the upper region
such be the interior of ullathorne court
but have thus describe it perhaps somewhat too tediously we beg to say that it be not the interior to which we wish to call the english tourist s attention though we advise he to lose no legitimate opportunity of become acquaint with it in a friendly manner
it be the outside of ullathorne that be so lovely
let the tourist get admission at least into the garden and fle himself on that soft award just opposite to the exterior angle of the house
he will there get the double frontage and enjoy that which be so lovely--the expanse of architectural beauty without the formal dullness of one long line
it be the colour of ullathorne that be so remarkable
it be all of that delicious tawny hue which no stone can give unless it have on it the vegetable richness of century
strike the wall with your hand and you will think that the stone have on it no covering but rub it carefully and you will find that the colour come off upon your finger
no colourist that ever yet work from a palette have be able to come up to this rich colouring of year crowd themselves on year
ullathorne be a high building for a country house for it possess three story and in each storey the window be of the same sort as that describe though vary in size and vary also in their line athwart the house
those of the ground floor be all uniform in size and position
but those above be irregular both in size and place and this irregularity give a bizarre and not unpicturesque appearance to the building
along the top on every side run a low parapet which nearly hide the roof and at the corner be more figure of fawn and satyr
such be ullathorne house
but we must say one word of the approach to it which shall include all the description which we mean to give of the church also
the picturesque old church of st ewold s stand immediately opposite to the iron gate which open into the court and be all but surround by the branch of lime tree which form the avenue lead up to the house from both side
this avenue be magnificent but it would lose much of its value in the eye of many proprietor by the fact that the road through it be not private property
it be a public lane between hedgerow with a broad grass margin on each side of the road from which the lime tree spring
ullathorne court therefore do not stand absolutely surround by its own ground though mr thorne be owner of all the adjacent land
this however be the source of very little annoyance to he
man when they be acquire property think much of such thing but they who live where their ancestor have live for year do not feel the misfortune
it never occur to either mr or miss thorne that they be not sufficiently private because the world at large might if it so wish walk or drive by their iron gate
that part of the world which avail itself of the privilege be however very small
such a year or two since be the thornes of ullathorne
such we believe be the inhabitant of many an english country home
may it be long before their number diminish
chapter xxiii mr arabin reads himself in at st ewold s on the sunday morning the archdeacon with his sister-in-law and mr arabin drive over to ullathorne as have be arrange
on their way thither the new vicar declare himself to be considerably disturb in his mind at the idea of thus face his parishioner for the first time
he have he say be always subject to mauvaise honte and an annoying degree of bashfulness which often unfit he for any work of a novel description and now he feel this so strongly that he fear he should acquit himself badly in st ewold s reading-desk
he know he say that those sharp little eye of miss thorne would be on to he and that they would not approve
all this the archdeacon greatly ridicule
he himself know not and have never know what it be to be shy
he could not conceive that miss thorne surround as she would be by the peasant of ullathorne and a few of the poor inhabitant of the suburb of barchester could in any way affect the composure of a man well accustom to address the learn congregation of st mary s at oxford and he laugh accordingly at the idea of mr arabin s modesty
thereupon mr arabin commence to subtilise
the change he say from st mary s to st ewold s be quite as powerful on the spirit as would be that from st ewold s to st mary s
would not a peer who by chance of fortune might suddenly be drive to herd among the navvy be as afraid of the jeer of his companion as would any navvy suddenly exalt to a seat among the peer
whereupon the archdeacon declare with a loud laugh that he would tell miss thorne that her new minister have liken she to a navvy
eleanor however pronounce such a conclusion unfair a comparison might be very just in its proportion which do not at all assimilate the thing compare
but mr arabin go in subtilise regard neither the archdeacon s raillery nor eleanor s defence
a young lady he say would execute with most perfect self-possession a difficult piece of music in a room crowd with stranger who would not be able to express herself in any intelligible language even on any ordinary subject and among her most intimate friend if she be require to do so stand on a box somewhat elevated among they
it be all an affair of education and he at forty find it difficult to educate himself now
eleanor dissent on the matter of the box and aver she could speak very well about dress or baby or leg of mutton from any box provide it be big enough for she to stand upon without fear even though all her friend be listen to she
the archdeacon be sure she would not be able to say a word but this prove nothing in favour of mr arabin
mr arabin say that he would try the question out with mrs bold and get she on a box some day when the rectory might be full of visitor
to this eleanor assent make condition that the visitor should be of their own set and the archdeacon cogitate in his mind whether by such a condition it be intend that mr slope should be include resolve also that if so the trial should certainly never take place in the rectory drawing-room at plumstead
and so arguing they drive up to the iron gate of ullathorne court
mr and miss thorne be stand ready dress for church in the hall and greet their clerical visitor with cordiality
the archdeacon be an old favourite
he be a clergyman of the old school and this recommend he to the lady
he have always be an opponent of free trade as long as free trade be an open question and now that it be no long so he be a clergyman have not be oblige like most of his lay tory companion to read his recantation
he could therefore be regard as a supporter of the immaculate fifty-three and be on this account a favourite with mr thorne
the little bell be tinkle and the rural population be stand about the lane lean on the church stile and against the wall of the old court anxious to get a look at their new minister as he pass from the house to the rectory
the archdeacon s servant have already precede they thither with the vestment
they all go together and when the lady pass into the church the three gentleman tarry a moment in the lane that mr thorne might name to the vicar with some kind of one-sided introduction the most leading among his parishioner
here be our churchwarden mr arabin farmer greenacre and mr stiles
mr stiles have the mill as you go into barchester and very good churchwarden they be not very severe i hope say mr arabin the two ecclesiastical officer touch their hat and each make a leg in the approved rural fashion assure the vicar that they be glad to have the honour of see he and add that the weather be very good for the harvest
mr stiles be a man somewhat verse in town life have an impression of his own dignity and do not quite like leave his pastor under the erroneous idea that he be a churchwarden keep the child in order during church time
twas thus he understand mr arabin s allusion to his severity and hasten to put matter right by observe that sexton clodheave look to the youngun and perhaps sometimes there maybe a thought too much stick go on during sermon mr arabin s bright eye twinkle as he catch that of the archdeacon and he smile to himself as he observe how ignorant his officer be of the nature of their authority and of the surveillance which it be their duty to keep even over himself
mr arabin read the lesson and preach
it be enough to put a man a little out let he have be ever so used to pulpit reading to see the knowing way in which the farmer cock their ear and set about a mental criticism as to whether their new minister do or do not fall short of the excellence of he who have lately depart from they
a mental and silent criticism it be for the exist moment but soon to be make public among the elder of st ewold s over the green graf of their child and forefather
the excellence however of poor old mr goodenough have not be wonderful and there be few there who do not deem that mr arabin do his work sufficiently well in spite of the slightly nervous affection which at first impede he and which nearly drive the archdeacon beside himself
but the sermon be the thing to try the man
it often surprise we that very young man can muster courage to preach for the first time to a strange congregation
man who be as yet little more than boy who have but just leave what indeed we may not call a school but a seminary intend for their tuition as scholar whose thought have be mostly of boat cricketing and wine party ascend a rostrum high above the head of the submissive crowd not that they may read god s word to those below but that they may preach their own word for the edification of their hearer
it seem strange to we that they be not stricken dumb by the new and awful solemnity of their position
how be i just turn twenty-three who have never yet pass then thoughtful day since the power of thought first come to i how be i to instruct these grey beard who with the weary thinking of so many year have approach so near the grave
can i teach they their duty
can i explain to they that which i so imperfectly understand that which year of study may have make so plain to they
have my newly acquire privilege as one of god s minister impart to i as yet any fitness for the wonderful work of a preacher
it must be suppose that such idea do occur to young clergyman and yet they overcome apparently with ease this difficulty which to we appear to be all but insurmountable
we have never be subject in the way of ordination to the power of a bishop s hand
it may be that there be in they something that sustain the spirit and banish the natural modesty of youth
but for ourselves we must own that the deep affection which dominie sampson feel for his young pupil have not more endear he to we than the bashful spirit which send he mute and inglorious from the pulpit when he rise there with the futile attempt to preach god s gospel
there be a rule in our church which forbid the young order of our clergyman to perform a certain portion of the service
the absolution must be read by a minister in priest s order
if there be no such minister present the congregation can have the benefit of no absolution but that which each may succeed in administer to himself
the rule may be a good one though the necessity for it hardly come home to the general understanding
but this forbearance on the part of youth would be much more appreciated if it be extend likewise to sermon
the only danger would be that the congregation would be too anxious to prevent their young clergyman from advance themselves to the rank of the ministry
clergymen who could not preach would be such blessing that they would be bribe to adhere to their incompetence
mr arabin however have not the modesty of youth to impede he and he succeed with his sermon even well than with the lesson
he take for his text two verse out of the second epistle of st john whosoever trangresseth and abideth not in the doctrine of christ hath not god
he that abideth in the doctrine of christ he hath both the father and son
if there come any unto you and bring you not this doctrine receive he not into your house neither bid he god speed he tell they that the house of theirs to which he allude be this their church in which he now address they for the first time that their most welcome and proper manner of bid he god speed would be their patient obedience to this teaching of the gospel but that he could put forward no claim to such conduct on their part unless he teach they the great christian doctrine of work and faith combine
on this he enlarge but not very amply and after twenty minute succeed in send his new friend home to their baked mutton and pudding well pleased with their new minister
then come the lunch at ullathorne
as soon as they be in the hall miss thorne take mr arabin s hand and assure he that she receive he into her house into the temple she say in which she worship and bid he god speed with all her heart
mr arabin be touch and squeeze the spinster s hand without utter a word in reply
then mr thorne express a hope that mr arabin find the church easy to fill and mr arabin have reply that he have no doubt he should do so as soon as he have learn to pitch his voice to the building they all sit down to the good thing before they
miss thorne take special care of mrs bold
eleanor still wear her widow s weed and therefore have about she that air of grave and sad maternity which be the lot of recent widow
this open the soft heart of miss thorne and make she look on her young guest as though too much could not be do for she
she heap chicken and ham upon her plate and pour out for she a full bumper of port wine
when eleanor who be not sorry to get it have drink a little of it miss thorne at once essay to fill it again
to this eleanor object but in vain
miss thorne wink and nod and whisper say that it be the proper thing and must be do and that she know all about it and so she desire mrs bold to drink it up and mind any body
it be your duty you know to support yourself she say into the ear of the young mother therebe more than yourself depend on it and thus she coshere up eleanor with cold fowl and port wine
how it be that poor man s wife who have no cold fowl and port wine on which to be coshere up nurse their child without difficulty whereas the wife of rich man who eat and drink everything that be good cannot do so we will for the present leave to the doctor and mother to settle between they
and then miss thorne be great about tooth
little johnny bold have be trouble for the last few day with his first incipient masticator and with that freemasonry which exist between lady miss thorne become aware of the fact before eleanor have half finish her wing
the old lady prescribe at once a receipt which have be much in vogue in the young day of her grandmother and warn eleanor with solemn voice against the fallacy of modern medicine
take his coral my dear say she and rub it well with carrot-juice rub it till the juice dry on it and then give it to he to play with-- but he havenot get a coral say eleanor
not get a coral say miss thorne with almost angry vehemence
not get a coral how can you expect that he should cut his tooth
have you get daffy s elixir eleanor explain that she have not
it have not be order by mr rerechild the barchester doctor whom she employ and then the young mother mention some shockingly modern succedaneum which mr rerechild s new light have teach he to recommend
miss thorne look awfully severe
take care my dear say she that the man know what he be about take care he donot destroy your little boy
but and her voice soften into sorrow as she say it and speak more in pity than in anger-- but i donot know who there be in barchester now that you can trust
poor dear old dr bumpwell indeed-- why miss thorne he die when i be a little girl yes my dear he do and an unfortunate day it be for barchester
as to those young man that have come up since mr rerechild by the by be quite as old as miss thorne herself one donot know where they come from or who they be or whether they know anything about their business or not i think there be very clever man in barchester say eleanor
perhaps there may be only i donot know they and itbe admit on all side that medical man benot now what they use to be
they use to be talente observe educate man
but now any whipper-snapper out of an apothecary s shop can call himself a doctor
i believe no kind of education be now think necessary eleanor be herself the widow of a medical man and feel a little inclined to resent all these hard saying
but miss thorne be so essentially good-natured that it be impossible to resent anything she say

at any rate my dear donot forget the carrot-juice and by all mean get he a coral at once
my grandmother thorne have the good tooth in the county and carry they to the grave with she at eighty
i have hear she say it be all the carrot-juice
she couldnot bear the barchester doctor
even poor dr bumpwell donot please she it clearly never occur to miss thorne that some fifty year ago dr bumpwell be only a rise man and therefore as much in need of character in the eye of the then lady of ullathorne as the present doctor be in her own
the archdeacon make a very good lunch and talk to his host about turnip-driller and new machine for reap while the host think it only polite to attend to a stranger and fear that perhaps he might not care about turnip crop on a sunday moot all manner of ecclesiastical subject

yes guano
i get it from bristol myself

he tell they it have become his duty to look about for a pastor for they to supply the place of one who have be long among they and that in this manner he regard as a son he whom he have select as st paul have regard the young disciple whom he send forth

chapter xxiv mr slope s manages matter very cleverly at puddingdale the next two week pass pleasantly enough at plumstead

it be singular how these emblem of grief fade away by unseen gradation
each pretend to be the counterpart of the forerunner and yet the last little bit of crimp white crape that sit so jauntily on the back of the head be as dissimilar to the first huge mountain of woe which disfigure the face of the weeper as the state of the hindoo be to the jointure of the english dowager

the party break up from breakfast in perfect harmony but fierce passion have arise before the evening which do not admit of their sitting at the same board for dinner
to explain this it will be necessary to go back a little
it will be remember that the bishop express to mr slope in his dressing-room his determination that mr quiverful should be confirm in his appointment to the hospital and that his lordship request mr slope to communicate this decision to the archdeacon
it will also be remember that the archdeacon have indignantly decline see mr slope and have instead write a strong letter to the bishop in which he all but demand the situation of warden for mr harding
to this letter the archdeacon receive an immediate formal reply from mr slope in which it be state that the bishop have receive and would give his good consideration to the archdeacon s letter
the archdeacon feel himself somewhat checkmate by this reply
what could he do with a man who would neither see he nor argue with he by letter and who have undoubtedly the power of appoint any clergyman he please
he have consult with mr arabin who have suggest the propriety of call in the aid of the master of lazarus
if say he you and dr gwynne formally declare your intention of wait upon the bishop the bishop will not dare to refuse to see you and if two such man as you see he together you will probably not leave he without carry your point the archdeacon do not quite like admit the necessity of his be back by the master of lazarus before he could obtain admission into the episcopal palace of barchester but still he feel that the advice be good and he resolve to take it
he write again to the bishop express a hope that nothing far would be do in the matter of the hospital till the consideration promise by his lordship have be give and then send off a warm appeal to his friend the master implore he to come to plumstead and assist in drive the bishop into compliance
the master have rejoin raise some difficulty but not decline and the archdeacon again press his point insist on the necessity for immediate action
dr gwynne unfortunately have the gout and could therefore name no immediate day but still agree to come if it should be finally find necessary
so the matter stand as regard the party at plumstead
but mr harding have another friend fight the battle for he quite as powerful as the master of lazarus and this be mr slope

what

either he or mrs proudie must go to the wall and now have come the time when he would try which it would be

mr quiverful be on the whole a worthy man

who can boast that he would have support such a burden with a different result
mr quiverful be an honest pain- staking drudge man anxious indeed for bread and meat anxious for mean to quiet his butcher and cover with return smile the now sour countenance of the baker s wife but anxious also to be right with his own conscience
he be not careful as another might be who sit on an easy worldly seat to stand well with those around he to shun a breath which might sully his name or a rumour which might affect his honour
he could not afford such nicety of conduct such moral luxury
