he have feel that his brother clergyman man whom he have know for the last twenty year look coldly on he from the first moment that he have show himself willing to sit at the foot of mr slope he have see that their look grow cold still when it become bruit about that he be to be the bishop s new warden at hiram s hospital
this be painful enough but it be the cross which he be doom to bear
he think of his wife whose last new silk dress be six year in wear
he think of all his young flock whom he could hardly take to church with he on sundays for there be not decent shoe and stocking for they all to wear
he think of the well-wear sleeve of his own black coat and of the stern face of the draper from whom he would fain ask for cloth to make another do he not know that the credit would be refuse he
then he think of the comfortable house in barchester of the comfortable income of his boy send to school of the girl with book in their hand instead of darn needle of his wife s face again cover with smile and of his daily board again cover with plenty
he think of all these thing and do thou also reader think of they and then wonder if thou canst that mr slope have appear to he to possess all those good gift which would grace a bishop s chaplain

he and i be nearly always of the same opinion on point of doctrine as well as church discipline and therefore i have have as his confidential chaplain very much in my own hand but i confess to you that i have a high ambition than to remain the chaplain of any bishop
there be no position in which more energy be now need than in those of our dean
the whole of our enormous cathedral establishment have be allow to go to sleep nay they be all but dead and ready for the sepulchre
and yet of what prodigious moment they might be make if as we intend they be so manage as to lead the way and show an example for all our parochial clergy
the bishop here be most anxious for my success indeed he go to-morrow to press the matter on the archbishop
i believe also i may count on the support of at least one of the most effective member of the government
but i confess the support of the jupiter if i be think worthy of it would be more gratifying to i than any other more gratifying if by it i should be successful and more gratifying also if although so support i should be unsuccessful
the time have in fact come in which no government can venture to fill up the high place of the church in defiance of the public press
the age of honourable bishop and noble dean have go by and any clergyman however humbly bear can now hope for success if his industry talent and character be sufficient to call forth the manifest opinion of the public in his favour
at the present moment we all feel that any counsel give in such matter by the jupiter have the great weight is indeed generally follow and we feel also--i be speak of clergyman of my own age and standing--that it should be so
there can be no patron less interested than the jupiter and none that more thoroughly understand the want of the people
i be sure you will not suspect i of ask from you any support which the paper with which you be connect cannot conscientiously give i
my object in writing be to let you know that i be a candidate for the appointment
it be for you to judge whether or no you can assist my view
i should not of course have write to you on such a matter have i not believe and i have have good reason so to believe that the jupiter approve of my view on ecclesiastical polity
the bishop express a fear that i may be consider too young for such a station my age be thirty-six
i cannot think that at the present day any hesitation need be feel on such a point
the public have lose its love for antiquated servant
if a man will ever be fit to do good work he will be fit at thirty-six year of age

chapter xxxiv oxford--the master and tutor of lazarus mr arabin as we have say have but a sad walk of it under the tree of plumstead churchyard
he do not appear to any of the family till dinner time and then he assume as far as their judgment go to be quite himself
he have as be his willnot ask himself a great many question and give himself a great many answer and the upshot of this be that he have set himself down for an ass
he have determine that he be much too old and much to rusty to commence the manouvre of lovemake that he have let the time slip through his hand which should have be use for such purpose and that now he must lie on his bed as he have make it
then he ask himself whether in truth he do love this woman and he answer himself not without a long struggle but at last honestly that he certainly do love she
he then ask himself whether he do not also love her money and he again answer himself that he do so
but here he do not answer honestly
it be and ever have be his weakness to look for impure motif for his own conduct
no doubt circumstance as he be with a small living and a fellowship accustom as he have be to collegiate luxury and expensive comfort he might have hesitate to marry a penniless woman have he feel ever so strong a predilection for the woman herself no doubt eleanor s fortune put all such difficulty out of the question but it be equally without doubt that his love for she have creep upon he without the slight idea on his part that he could ever benefit his own condition by share her wealth
when he have stand on the hearth-rug count the pattern and count also the future chance of his own life the remembrance of mrs bold s comfortable income have not certainly damp his first assure feeling of love for she
and why should it have do so
need it have do so with the purest of man
be that as it may mr arabin decide against himself he decide that it have do so in his case and that he be not the pure of man
he also decide which be more to his purpose that eleanor do not care a straw for he and that very probably do not care a straw for his rival
then he make up his mind not to think of she any more and go on thinking of she till he be almost in a state to drown himself in the little brook which be at the bottom of the archdeacon s ground
and ever and again his mind would revert to the signora neroni and he would make comparison between she and eleanor bold not always in favour of the latter
the signora have listen to he and flatter he and believe in he at least she have tell he so

but he never could have love the signora neroni as he feel that he now love eleanor
and so he fling stone into the brook instead of fling in himself and sit down on its margin as sad a gentleman as you shall meet in a summer s day
he hear the dinner-bell ring from the churchyard and he know that it be time to recover his self possession
he feel that he be disgrace himself in his own eye that he have be idle his time and neglect the high duty which he have take upon himself to perform
he should have spend the afternoon among the poor at st ewold s instead of wander about plumstead an ancient love-lorn swain dejected and sighing full of imaginary sorrow and wertherian grief
he be thoroughly ashamed of himself and determine to lose no time in retrieve his character so damage in his own eye
thus when he appear at dinner he be as animate as ever and be the author of most of the conversation which grace the archdeacon s board on that evening

mr arabin strongly advise that mr harding should take no step regard the hospital in connexion with or in consequence of mr slope s letter

be mr harding to present himself at the palace he might merely be play mr slope s game and thus it be settle that nothing should be do till the great dr gwynne s arrival or at any rate without that potentate s sanction
it be droll how these man talk of mr harding as though he be a puppet and plan their intrigue and small ecclesiastical manouvre without dream of take he into their confidence
there be a comfortable house and income in question and it be very desirable and certainly very just that mr harding should have they but that at present be not the main point it be expedient to beat the bishop and if possible to smash mr slope
mr slope have set up or be suppose to have set up a rival candidate
of all thing the most desirable would have be to have have mr quiverful s appointment publish to the public and then annul by the clamour of an indignant world loud in the defence of mr harding s right
but of such an event the chance be small a slight fraction only of the world would be indignant and that fraction would be one not accustomed to loud speaking
and then the preferment have in a sort of way be offer to mr harding and have in a sort of way be refuse by he
mr slope s wicked cunning hand have be peculiarly conspicuous in the way in which this have be bring to pass and it be the success of mr slope s cunning which be so painfully grate the feeling of the archdeacon
that which of all thing he most dread be that he should be out-generalle by mr slope and just at present it appear probable that mr slope would turn his flank steal a march on he cut off his provision carry his strong town by a coup de main and at last beat he thoroughly in a regular pitch battle
the archdeacon feel that his flank have be turn when desire to wait on mr slope instead of the bishop that a march have be steal when mr harding be induce to refuse the bishop s offer that his provision would be cut off when mr quiverful get the hospital that eleanor be the strong town doom to be take and that mr slope as dean of barchester would be regard by all the world as the conqueror in that final conflict
dr gwyinne be the deus ex machina who be to come down upon the barchester stage and bring about deliverance from these terrible evil
but how can melodramatic denoument be properly bring about how can vice and mr slope be punish and virtue and the archdeacon be reward while the avenge god be lay up with the gout
in the mean time evil may be triumphant and poor innocence transfix to the earth by an arrow from dr proudie s quiver may be dead upon the ground not to be resuscitate even by dr gwynne
two or three day after eleanor s departure mr arabin go to oxford and soon find himself closet with the august head of his college
it be quite clear that dr gwynne be not very sanguine as to the effect of his journey to barchester and not over anxious to interfere with the bishop
he have have the gout but be very nearly convalescent and mr arabin at once see that have the mission be one of which the master thoroughly approve he would before this have be at plumstead
as it be dr gwynne be resolve to visit his friend and willingly promise to return to barchester with mr arabin
he could not bring himself to believe that there be any probability that mr slope would be make dean of barchester
rumour he say have reach even his ear not at all favourable to that gentleman s character and he express himself strongly of the opinion that any such appointment be quite out of the question
at this stage of the proceeding the master s right-hand man tom staple be call in to assist at the conference
tom staple be the tutor of lazarus and moreover a great man at oxford
though universally know by a specie of nomenclature as very undignified
tom staple be one who maintain a high dignity in the university
he be as it be the leader of the oxford tutor a body of man who consider themselves collectively as be by very little if at all second in importance to the head themselves
it be not always the case that the master or warden or provost or principal can hit it off exactly with his tutor
a tutor be by no mean indispose to have a will of his own
but at lazarus they be great friend and firm ally at the time of which we be write
tom staple be a hale strong man of about forty-five short in stature swarthy in face with strong sturdy black hair and crisp black beard of which very little be allow to show itself in the shape of whisker
he always wear a white neckcloth clean indeed but not tie with that scrupulous care which now distinguish some of our young clergy
he be of course always clothe in a seemly suit of solemn black
mr staple be a decent cleanly liver not over addicted to any sensuality but nevertheless a somewhat warmish hue be begin to adorn his nose the peculiar effect as his friend aver of a certain pipe of port introduce into the cellar of lazarus the very same year in which the tutor enter in as a freshman
there be also perhaps with a little redolence of port wine as it be the slight possible twang in mr staple s voice
in these day tom staple be not a very happy man university reform have long be his bugbear and now be his bane
it be not with he as with most other an affair of politic respect which when the need exist he could for party sake or on behalf of principle maintain a certain amount of necessary zeal it be not with he a subject for dilettante warfare and courteous common-place opposition
to he it be life and death
he would willingly have be a martyr in the cause have the cause admit of martyrdom
at the present day unfortunately public affair will allow of no martyr and therefore it be that there be such a deficiency of zeal
could gentleman of l 10 000 a year have die on their own door-step in defence of protection no doubt some half-dozen glorious old baronet would have so fall and the school of protection would at this day have be crowd with scholar
who can fight strenuously in any combat in which there be no danger
tom staple would have willingly be impale before a committee of the house could he by such self-sacrifice have infuse his own spirit into the component member of the hebdomadal board
tom staple be one of those who in his heart approve of the credit system which have of old be in vogue between the student and tradesman of the university
he know and acknowledge to himself that it be useless in these degenerate day publicly to contend with the jupiter on such a subject
the jupiter have undertake to rule the university and tom staple be well aware that the jupiter be too powerful for he
but in secret and among his safe companion he would argue that the system of credit be an ordeal good for young man to undergo
the bad man say he and the weak and worthless blunder into danger and burn their foot but the good man they who have any character they who have that within they which can reflect credit in their alma mater they come through scatheless
what merit will there be to a young man to get through safely if he guard and protect and restrain like a school-boy
by so doing the period of the ordeal be only postpone and the manhood of the man will be defer from the age of twenty to that of twenty-four
if you bind he with leading-string at college he will break loose while eat for the bar in london bind he there and he will break loose afterwards when he be a married man
the wild oats must be sow somewhere
twas thus that tom staple would argue of young man not indeed with much consistency but still with some practical knowledge of the subject gather from long experience
and now tom staple proffer such wisdom as he have for the assistance of dr gwynne and mr arabin
quite out of the question say he argue that mr slope could not possibly be make the new dean of barchester
so i think say the master
he have no standing and if all i hear be true very little character as to character say tom staple i donot think much of that
they rather like loose parson for dean a little fast living or a dash of infidelity be no bad recommendation to a cathedral close
but they couldnot make mr slope the last two dean have be cambridge man youwill not show i an instance of their making three man run from the same university
we donot get out share and never shall i suppose but we must at least have one out of the three these sort of rule be all go out by now say mr arabin
everything have go by i believe say tom staple
the cigar have be smoke out and we be the ash speak for yourself staple say the master
i speak for all say the tutor stoutly
it be come to that that there will be no life leave anywhere in the country
no one be any long fit to rule himself or those belong to he
the government be to find we all in everything and the press be to find the government
nevertheless mr slope willnot be dean of barchester and who will be the warden of the hospital say mr arabin
i hear that mr quiverful be already appoint say tom staple
i think not say the master
and i think moreover that dr proudie will not be so short-sighted as to run against such a rock mr slope should himself have sense enough to prevent it but perhaps mr slope may have no objection to see his patron on a rock say the suspicious tutor
what could he get by that ask mr arabin
it be impossible to see the double of such a man say mr staple
it seem quite clear that bishop proudie be altogether in his hand and it be equally clear that he have be move heaven and earth to get this mr quiverful into the hospital although he must know that such an appointment would be most damaging to the bishop
it be impossible to understand such a man and dreadful to think add mr staple sigh deeply that the welfare and fortune of good man may depend on his intrigue dr gwynne or mr staple be not in the least aware nor even be mr arabin that this mr slope of whom they be talk have be use his utmost effort to put their own candidate into the hospital and that in lieu of be a permanent in the palace his own expulsion therefrom have be already decide on by the high power of the diocese
iwill tell you what say the tutor if this quiverful be thrust into the hospital and dr trefoil must die i should not wonder if the government be to make mr harding dean of barchester
they would feel bound to do something for he after all that be say when he resign dr gwynne at the moment make no reply to this suggestion but it do not the less impress itself on his mind
if mr harding could not be warden of the hospital why should he not be dean of barchester
and so the conference end without any very fix resolution and dr gwynne and mr arabin prepare for their journey to plumstead on the morrow
chapter xxxv miss thorne s fete champetre the day of the ullathorne party arrive and all the world be there or at least so much of the world as have be include in miss thorne s invitation
as we have say the bishop return home on the previous evening and on the same evening and by the same train come dr gwynne and mr arabin from oxford
the archdeacon with his brougham be in wait for the master of lazarus so that there be a goodly show of church dignitary on the platform of the railway

he have receive that morning a civil note from sir nicholas fitzwiggin not promise much indeed but then mr slope know or fancy that he know that it be not etiquette for government officer to make promise
though sir nicholas promise nothing he imply a good deal declare his conviction that mr slope would make an excellent dean and wish he every kind of success

he have fully make up his mind to throw himself and his fortune at the widow s foot and have almost determined to select the present propitious morning for do so
the signora have of late be less than civil to he
she have indeed admit his visit and listen at any rate without anger to his love but she have torture he and revile he jeered at he and ridicule he while she allow he to call she the most beautiful of live woman to kiss her hand and to proclaim himself with reiterate oath her adorer her slave and worshipper
miss thorne be in great perturbation yet in great glory on the morning of this day
mr thorne also though the party be none of his giving have much heavy work on his hand
but perhaps the most overtaske the most anxious and the most effective of all the ullathorne household be mr plomacy the steward
this last personage have in the time of mr thorne s father when the directory hold dominion in france go over to paris with letter in his boot heel for some of the royal party and such have be his good luck that he have return safe
he have then be very young and be now very old but the exploit give he a character for political enterprise and secret discretion which still avail he as thoroughly as it have do in its fresh gloss
mr plomacy have be steward of ullathorne for more than fifty year and a very easy life he have have of it
who could require much absolute work from a man who have carry safely at his heel that which if discover would have cost he his head
consequently mr plomacy have never work hard and of latter year have never work at all
he have a taste for timber and therefore he mark the tree that be to be cut down he have a taste for gardening and would therefore allow no shrub to be plant or bed to be make without his express sanction
in these matter he be sometimes drive to run counter to his mistress but he rarely allow his mistress to carry the point against he
but on occasion such as the present mr pomney come out strong
he have the honour of the family at heart he thoroughly appreciate the duty of hospitality and therefore when gala doing be go on always take the management into his own hand and reign supreme over master and mistress
to give mr pomney his due old as he be he thoroughly understand such work as he have in hand and do it well
the order of the day be to be as follow
the quality as the upper class in rural district be designate by the low with so much true discrimination be to eat a breakfast and the non-quality be to eat a dinner
two marquee have be erect for these two banquet that for the quality on the esoteric or garden side of a certain deep ha-ha and that for the non-quality on the exoteric or paddock side of the same
both be of huge dimension that on the outer side one may say on an egregious scale but mr pomney declare that neither would be sufficient
to remedy this an auxiliary banquet be prepare in the dining-room and a subsidiary board be to be spread sub dio for the accommodation of the low class of yokel on the ullathorne property
no one who have not have a hand in the preparation of such an affair can understand the manifold difficulty which miss thorne encounter in her project
have she not be make throughout of the very fine whalebone rivette with the good yorkshire steel she must have sink under they
have not mr pomney feel how much be justly expect from a man who at one time carry the destiny of europe in his boot he would have give way and his mistress so desert must have perish among her pole and canvass
in the first place there be a dreadful line to be draw
who be to dispose themselves within the ha-ha and who without
to this the unthinking will give an off-hand answer as they will to every ponderous question
oh the bishop and such like within the ha-ha and farmer greenacre and such without
true my unthinke friend but who shall define these such-like
it be in such definition that the whole difficulty of society consist
to seat the bishop on an arm chair on the lawn and place farmer greenacre at the end of a long table in the paddock be easy enough but where will you put mrs lookaloft whose husband though a tenant on the estate hunt in a red coat whose daughter go to a fashionable seminary in barchester who call her farm house rosebank and who have a pianoforte in her drawing-room
the misses lookaloft as they call themselves willnot sit content among the bumpkin
mrs lookaloft willnot squeeze her fine clothe on a bench and talk familiarly about cream and duckling to good mrs greenacres
and yet mrs lookaloft be not fit companion and never have be the associate of the thornes and the grantlys
and if mrs lookaloft be admit within the sanctum of fashionable life if she be allow with her three daughter to leap the ha-ha why not the wife and daughter of other family also
mrs greenacre be at present well content with the paddock but she might cease to be so if she see mrs lookaloft on the lawn
and thus poor miss thorne have a hard time of it
and how be she to divide the guest between the marquee and the parlour
she have a countess come and honourable john and an honourable george and a whole bevy of ladies amelia rosina margaretta c she have a leash of baronet with their baroness and as we all know a bishop
if she put they on the lawn no one would go into the parlour if she put they into the parlour no one would go into the tent
she think of keep the old people in the house and leave the lawn to the lover
she might as well have seat herself at once in a hornet s nest
mr pomney know well than this
bless your soul ma am say he there willnot be no old lady not one bar yourself and old mrs chantantrum personally miss thorne accept this distinction in her favour as a compliment to her good sense but nevertheless she have no desire to be closet on the come occasion with mrs chantantrum
she give up all idea of any arbitrary division of her guest and determine if possible to put the bishop on the lawn and the countess in the house to sprinkle the baronet and thus divide the attraction
what to do with the lookalofts even mr plomacy could not decide
they must take their chance
they have be specially tell in the invitation that all the tenant have be invite and they might probably have the good sense to stay away if they object to mix with the rest of the tenantry
then mr plomacy declare his apprehension that the honourable johns and honourable georges would come in a sort of amphibious costume half morning half evening satin neckhandkerchief frock coat primrose glove and polished boot and that be so dressed they would decline rid at the quintain or take part in any of the athletic game which miss thorne have prepare with so much care
if the lord johns and lord georges donot ride at the quintain miss thorne might be sure that nobody else would
but say she in dolorous voice all but overcome by her care it be specially signify that there be to be sport and so there will be of course say mr pomney
theywill all be sport with the young lady in the laurel walk
theybe the sport they care most about now-a-day
if you get the young man at the quintain youwill have all the young woman in the pout cannot they look on as their great grandmother do before they say miss thorne
it seem to i that the lady benot content with look now-a-day
whatever the man do theywill do
if youwill have side saddle on the nag and let they go at the quintain too itwill answer capital no doubt miss thorne make no reply
she feel that she have no good ground on which to defend her sex of the present generation from the sarcasm of mr pomney
she have once declare in one of her warm moment that now-a-day the gentleman be all woman and the lady all man she could not alter the debase character of the age
but such be the case why should she take on herself to cater for the amusement of people of such degraded taste
this question she ask herself more than once and she could only answer herself with a sigh
there be her own brother wilfred on whose shoulder rest the all the ancient honour of ullathorne house it be very doubtful whether even he would consent to go at the quintain as mr pomney not injudiciously express it
and now the morning arrive
the ullathorne household be early on the move
cook be cook in the kitchen long before daylight and man be drag out table and hammer red baize on to bench at the early dawn
with what dread eagerness do miss thorne look out at the weather as soon as the parting veil of night permit she to look at all
in this respect at any rate there be nothing to grieve she
the glass have be rise for the last three day and the morning break with that dull chill steady grey haze which in autumn generally presage a clear and dry day
by seven she be dress and down
miss thorne know nothing of the modern luxury of deshabille
she would as soon have think of appear before her brother without her stocking as without her stay and miss thorne s stay be no trifle
and yet there be nothing for she to do when down
she fidget out to the lawn and then back into the kitchen
she put on her high-heeled clog and fidget out into the paddock
then she go into the small home park where the quintain be erect
the pole and cross-bar and the swivel and the target and the bag of flour be all complete
she get up on a carpenter s bench and touch the target with her hand it go round with beautiful ease the swivel have be oil to perfection
she almost wish to take old plomacy at his word to go on a side saddle and have a tilt at it herself
what must a young man be think she who could prefer maunder among the tree with a wishy-washy school girl to such fun as this
well say she aloud to herself one man can take a horse to water but a thousand cannot make he drink
there it be
if they havenot the spirit to enjoy it the fault shallnot be mine and so she return the house
at a little after eight her brother come down and they have a sort of scrap breakfast in his study
the tea be make without the customary urn and they dispense with the usual roll and toast
eggs be also miss for every egg in the parish have be whip into custard bake into pie or boil into lobster salad

i have be look at the quintain wilfred say she and it appear to be quite right oh ah yes say he
it seem to be so yesterday when i see it mr thorne be begin to be rather bored by his sister s love of sport and have especially no affection for this quintain post
i wish youwould just try it after breakfast say she
you could have the saddle put on mark antony and the pole be there all handy
you can take the flour bag off you know if you think mark antony willnot be quick enough add miss thorne see that her brother s countenance be not indicative of complete accordance with her little proposition
now mark antony be a valuable old hunter excellently suit to mr thorne s usual requirement steady indeed at his fence but extremely sure very good in deep ground and safe on the road
but he have never yet be ride at a quintain and mr thorne be not inclined to put he to the trial either with or without the bag of flour
he hum and hawed and finally declare that he be afraid mark antony would shy
then try the cob say the indefatigable miss thorne
hebe in physic say wilfred
therebe the beelzebub colt say his sister i know hebe in the stable because i see peter exercise he just now my dear monica hebe so wild that itbe as much as i can do to manage he at all
hewould destroy himself and i too if i attempt to ride he at such a rattletrap as that a rattletrap
the quintain that she have put up with so much anxious care the game that she have prepare for the amusement of the stalwart yeoman of the country the sport that have be honour by the affection of so many of their ancestor
it cut she to the heart to hear it so denominate by her own brother
there be but the two of they leave together in the world and it have ever be one of the rule by which miss thorne have regulate her conduct through life to say nothing that could provoke her brother

now however she be greatly try
the colour mount to her ancient cheek and the fire blaze in her still bright eye but yet she say nothing

and why should she expect her brother to do that which her brother s guest would decline to do
some dim faint idea of the impracticability of her own view flit across her brain
perhaps it be necessary that race doom to live on the same soil should give way to each other and adopt each other s pursuit
perhaps it be impossible that after more than five century of close intercourse normans should remain normans and saxons saxons
perhaps after all her neighbour be wise than herself such idea do occasionally present themselves to miss thorne s mind and make she sad enough
but it never occur to she that her favourite quintain be but a modern copy of a norman knight s amusement an adaptation of the noble tourney to the taste and habit of the saxon yeoman
of this she be ignorant and it would have be cruelty to instruct she

but his intention be equally good and whenever he find that he have forget they it be a matter of grief to he
my dear monica say he i beg your pardon i donot in the least mean to speak ill of the game

you know you always forget that i an t a young man i be quite sure you be not an old man wilfred say she accept the apology in her heart and smile at he with the tear still on her cheek

this be farmer greenacre s eld son who to tell the truth have from his early year take the exact measure of miss thorne s foot

at about nine the low order begin to congregate in the paddock and park under the surveillance of mr plomacy and the head gardener and head groom who be swear in as his deputy and be to assist he in keep the peace and promote the sport
many of the young inhabitant of the neighbourhood think that they could not have too much of a good thing have come at a very early hour and the road between the house and the church have be throng for some time before the gate be throw open

mr plomacy however well know who be welcome and who be not
to some even though uninvited he allow ingress
donot be too particular plomacy his mistress have say especially with the child
if they live anywhere near let they in act on this hint mr plomacy do let in many an eager urchin and a few tidily dress girl with their swain who in no way belong to the property
but to the denizen of the city he be inexorable
many a barchester apprentice make his appearance there that day and urge with piteous supplication that he have be work all the week in make saddle and boot for the use of ullathorne in compound dos for the horse or cut up carcass for the kitchen
no such claim be allow
mr plomacy know nothing about the city apprentice he be to admit the tenant and labourer on the estate miss thorne benot go to take in the whole city of barchester and so on
nevertheless before the day be half over all this be find to be useless
almost anybody who choose to come make his way into the park and the care of the guardian be transfer to the table on which the banquet be spread
even here there be many an unauthorized claimant for a plate of whom it be impossible to get quit without some commotion than the place and food be worth
chapter xxxvi ullathorne sports--act i the trouble in civilised life of entertaining company as it be call too generally without much regard to strict veracity be so great that it cannot but be matter of wonder that people be so fond of attempt it
it be difficult to ascertain what be the quid pro quo
if they who give such laborious party and who endure such toil and turmoil in the vain hope of give they successfully really enjoy the party give by other the matter would be understand
a sense of justice would induce man and woman to undergo in behalf of other those misery which other have undergo on their behalf
but they all profess that go out be as great a bore as receive and to look at they when they be out one cannot but believe they
entertain
who shall have sufficient self-assurance who shall feel sufficient confidence in his own power to dare to boast that he can entertain his company
a clown can sometimes do so and sometimes a dancer in short petticoat and stuff pink leg occasionally perhaps a singer
but beyond these success in this art of entertaining be not often achieve
young man and girl link themselves kind with kind pair like bird in spring because nature will it they after a simple fashion do entertain each other
few other even try
lady when they open their house modestly confess it may be presume their own incapacity mainly trust to wax candle and upholstery
gentleman seem to rely on their white waistcoat
to these be add for the delight of the more sensual champagne and such good thing of the table as fashion allow to be still consider as comestible
even in this respect the world be deteriorate
all the good soup be now taboo and at the house of one s accustomed friend small barrister doctor government clerk and such like for we cannot all of we always live as grandee surround by an elysium of livery servant one get a cold potato hand to one as a sort of finale to one s slice of mutton
alas
for those happy day when one could say to one s neighbourhood jones shall i give you some mashed turnip--may i trouble you for a little cabbage and then the pleasure of drink wine with mrs jones and miss smith with all the joneses and all the smiths
these latter-day habit be certainly more economical
miss thorne however boldly attempt to leave the modern beat track and make a positive effort to entertain her guest
alas
she do so with but moderate success
they have all their own way of go and would not go her way
she pip to they but they would not dance
she offer to they good honest household cake make of currant and flour and egg and sweetmeat but they would feed themselves on trashy wafer from the shop of the barchester pastry-cook on chalk and gum and adulterate sugar
poor miss thorne
yours be not the first honest soul that have vainly strive to recall the glory of happy day go by
if fashion suggest to a lady de courcy that when invite to a dejeuner at twelve o clock she ought to come at three no eloquence of thine will teach she the advantage of a near approach to punctuality
she have fondly think that when she call on her friend to come at twelve and especially beg they to believe that she mean it she would be able to see they comfortably seat in their tent at two
vain woman--or rather ignorant woman--ignorant of the advance of that civilization which the world have witness while she be grow old

the art of do this be among the most precious of those usually cultivate by person who know how to live
there be no withstand it
who can go systematically to work and have do battle with the primary accusation and settle that then bring forward a counter-charge and support that also

his very strength be his weakness his very weakness be his strength
the one be never prepare for combat the other be always ready
therefore it be that in this world the man that be in the wrong almost invariably conquer the man that be in the right and invariably despise he

chapter xxxix the lookalofts and the greenacres on the whole miss thorne s provision for the amusement and feeding of the outer class in the exoteric paddock be not unsuccessful
two little drawback to the general happiness do take place but they be of a temporary nature and apparent rather than real
the first be the downfall of young harry greenacre and the other be the uprise of mrs lookaloft and her family
as to the quintain it become more popular among the boy on foot than it would ever have be among the man on horseback even have young greenacre be more successful
it be twirl round and round till it be nearly twist out of the ground and the bag of flour be use with great gusto in powder the back and head of all who could be coax within the vicinity
of course it be report all throughout the assemblage that harry be dead and there be a pathetic scene between he and his mother when it be find that he have escape scatheless from the fall
a good deal of beer be drunk on the occasion and the quintain be dratte and bother and very generally anathematise by all the mother who have young son likely to be place in similar jeopardy
but the affair of mrs lookaloft be of a more serious nature
i do tell ee plainly face to face--she be there in madam s drawing-room herself and gussy and they two walloping gal dress up to their very eyes this be say by a very positive very indignant and very fat farmer s wife who be sit on the end of a bench lean on the handle of a huge cotton umbrella
but you donot zee she dame guffern say mrs greenacres whom this information join to the recent peril undergone by her son almost overpowered
mr greenacres hold just as much land as mr lookaloft pay his rent quite as punctually and his opinion in the vestry-room be reckon to be every whit as good
mrs lookaloft s rise in the world have be wormwood to mrs greenacre
she have not taste herself for the sort of finery which convert barleystubb farm into rosebank and which have occasionally grace mr lookaloft s letter with the dignity of esquirehood

it be just now five year since we call the attention of our reader to the quiet city of barchester
from that day to this we have in no way meddle with the affair of that happy ecclesiastical community
since then an old bishop have die there and a young bishop have be instal but we believe we do not do more than give some customary record of the interesting event

it will be remember that at the time allude to now five year past we have occasion to remark on the state of a charity at barchester call hiram s hospital
we think that it be maladministered and that the very estimable and reverend gentleman who hold the office of warden be somewhat too highly pay for duty which be somewhat too easily perform
this gentleman--and we say it in all sincerity and with no touch of sarcasm--have never look on the matter in this light before
we do not wish to take praise to ourselves whether praise be due or not
but the consequence of our remark be that the warden do look into the matter and find on do so that he himself could come to no other opinion than that express by we he very creditably throw up the appointment
the then bishop then as creditably decline to fill the vacancy till the affair be put on a well footing
parliament then take it up and we have now the satisfaction of inform our reader that hiram s hospital will be immediately re-open under new auspex
heretofore provision be make for the maintenance of twelve old man
this will now be extend to the fair sex and twelve elderly woman if any such can be find in barchester will be add to the establishment

the warden for there will still be a warden will receive an income more in keep with the extent of the charity than that heretofore pay
the stipend we believe will be l 450
we may add that the excellent house which the former warden inhabit will still be attach to the situation
barchester hospital cannot perhaps boast a world-wide reputation but as we advertise to its state of decadence we think it right also to advert to its renaissance

but while we be on the subject of barchester we will venture with all respectful humility to express our opinion on another matter connect with the ecclesiastical polity of that ancient city
dr trefoil the dean die yesterday
a short record of his death give his age and the various piece of preferment which he have at different time hold will be find in another column in this paper
the only fault we know in he be his age and as that be a crime of which we may all hope to be guilty we will not bear heavily on it
may he rest in peace
but though the great age of an expire dean cannot be make matter of reproach we be not inclined to look on such a fault as at all pardonable in a dean just bring to the birth
we do hope the day of sexagenarian appointment be past
if we want dean we must want they for some purpose
that purpose will necessarily be well fulfil by a man of forty than by a man of sixty
if we be to pay dean at all we be to pay they for some sort of work
that work be it what it may will be well perform by a workman in the prime of life
dr trefoil we see be eighty when he die
as we have as yet complete no plan for position superannuated clergyman we do not wish to get rid of any exist dean of that age
but we prefer have as few such as possible
if a man of seventy be now appoint we beg to point out to lord--that he will be past all use in a year or two if indeed he be not so at the present moment
his lordship will allow we to remind he that all man be not evergreen like himself
we hear that mr slope s name have be mention for this preferment
mr slope be at present chaplain to the bishop
a well man could hardly be select
he be a man of talent young active and conversant with the affair of the cathedral he be moreover we conscientiously believe a truly pious clergyman
we know that his service in the city of barchester have be highly appreciate
he be an eloquent preacher and a ripe scholar
such a selection as this would go far to raise the confidence of the public in the present administration of church patronage and would teach man to believe that from henceforth the establishment of our church will not afford easy couch to wear-out clerical voluptuary stand at a reading-desk in the barchester news-room mr slope digest this article with considerable satisfaction

chapter li mr slope bids farewell to the palace and its inhabitant we must now take leave of mr slope and of the bishop and of mrs proudie
these leave-taking in novel be as disagreeable as they be in real life not so sad indeed for they want the reality of sadness but quite as perplexing and generally less satisfactory
what novelist what fielding what scott what george sand or sue or duncan can impart an interest to the last chapter of his fictitious history
promise of two child and superhuman happiness be of no avail nor assurance of extreme respectability carry to an age far exceed that usually allot to mortal
the sorrow of our hero and heroine they be you delight oh public
their sorrow or their sin or their absurdity not their virtue good sense and consequent reward
when we begin to tint our final page with couleur de rise as in accordance with fix rule we must do we altogether extinguish our own power of pleasing
when we become dull we offend your intellect and we must become dull or we should offend your taste
a late writer wish to sustain his interest to the last page hang his hear at the end of the third volume
the consequence be that no one should read his novel
and who can apportion out and dovetail his incident dialogue character and descriptive morsel so as to fit they all exactly into 439 page without either compress they unnaturally or extend they artificially at the end of his labour
do i not myself know that i be at this moment in want of a dozen page and that i be sick with cudgel my brain to find they
and then when everything be do the kindest-hearted critic of they all invariably twit we with the incompetency and lameness of our conclusion
we have either become idle and neglect it or tedious and over-labour it
it be insipid or unnatural over-strained or imbecile
it mean nothing or attempt too much
the last scene of all as all last scene we fear must be be second childishness and mere oblivion sans tooth san eye san taste san everything i can only say that if some critic who thoroughly know his work and have labour on it till experience have make he perfect will write the last fifty page of a novel in the way they should be write
i for one will in future do my good to copy the example
guide by my own light only i despair of success

but
but-- say mr harding and then fail to get any far he look imploringly at the archdeacon
the truth be arabin say the doctor that after all you be not destine to be the son-in-law to a dean
nor be i either morebe the pity mr arabin look to he for explanation
be not mr harding to be the new dean it appear not say the archdeacon
mr arabin s face fall a little and he look from one to the other
it be plainly to be see from they both that there be no cause for unhappiness in the matter at least not of an unhappiness to they but there be as yet no clarification of the mystery
think how old i be say mr harding imploringly
fiddlestick say the archdeacon
thatbe all very well but it willnot make a young man of i say mr harding
and who be to be the dean ask mr arabin
yes that be the question say the archdeacon
come mr precentor since you obstinately refuse to be anything else let we know who be to be the man
he have get the nomination in his pocket with eye brim full of tear mr harding pull out the letter and hand it to his future son-in-law
he try to make a little speech but fail altogether
have give up the document he turn round to the wall feign to blow his nose and then sit himself down on the old dean s dingy horse-hair sofa
and here we find it necessary to bring our account of the interview to an end
nor can we pretend to describe the rapture with which mr harding be receive by his daughter
she weep with grief and with joy with grief that her father should in his old age still be without that rank and worldly position which accord to her idea he have so well earn and with joy that he her darling father should have bestow on that other dear one the good thing of which he himself would not open his hand to take possession
and she mr harding again show his weakness
in the melee of the exposal of their love and reciprocal affection he find himself unable to resist the entreaty of all party that his lodging in the high street should be give up
eleanor would not live in the deanery she say unless her father live there also
mr arabin would not be dean unless mr harding would be co-dean with he
the archdeacon declare that his father-in-law should not have his own way in everything and mrs grantly carry he off to plumstead that he might remain there till mr and mrs arabin be in a state to receive he at their own mansion
press by such argument as these what could a weak man do but yield
but there be yet another task which it behove mr harding to do before he could allow himself to be at rest
little have be say in these page of the state of those remain old man who have live under his sway at the hospital
but not on this account must it be presume that he have forget they or that in their state of anarchy and in their want of government he have omit to visit they
he visit they constantly and have latterly give they to understand that they would soon be require to subscribe their adherence to a new master
there be now but five of they one of they not have be but quite lately carry to his rest--but five of the full number which have hitherto be twelve and which be now to be raise to twenty-four include woman
of these old bunce who for many year have be the favourite of the late warden be one and abel handy who have be the humble mean from drive that warden from his home be another
mr harding now resolve that he himself would introduce the new warden to the hospital
he feel that many circumstance might conspire to make the man receive mr quiverful with aversion and disrespect he feel also that mr quiverful might himself feel some qualm of conscience if he enter the hospital with an idea that he do so in hostility to his predecessor
mr harding therefore determine to walk in arm in arm with mr quiverful and to ask from these man their respectful obedience to their new master
on return to barchester he find that mr quiverful have not yet sleep in the hospital house or enter on his new duty
he accordingly make known to that gentleman his wish and his proposition be not reject
it be a bright clear morning though in november that mr harding and mr quiverful arm in arm walk through the hospital gate
it be one trait in our old friend s character that he do nothing with parade
he omit even in the more important doing of his life that sort of parade by which most of we deem it necessary to grace our important doing
we have housewarming christening and gala day we keep if not our own birthday those of our child we be apt to fuss ourselves if call upon to change our residence and have almost all of we our little state occasion
mr harding have no state occasion
when he leave his old house he go forth from it with the same quiet composure as though he be merely take his daily walk and now that he re-enter it with another warden under his wing he do so with the same quiet step and calm demeanour
he be a little less upright than he have be five year nay it be nearly six year ago he walk perhaps a little slow his footfall be perhaps a thought less firm otherwise one might have same that he be merely return with a friend under his arm
this friendliness be everything to mr quiverful
to he even in his poverty the thought that he be supplant a brother clergyman so kind and courteous as mr harding have be very bitter
under his circumstance it have be impossible for he to refuse the proffered boon he could not reject the bread that be offer to his child or refuse to ease the heavy burden that have so long oppress that poor wife of his nevertheless it have be very grievous to he to think that in go to the hospital he might encounter the ill will of his brother in the diocese
all this mr harding have fully comprehend
it be for such feeling as these for the nice comprehension of such motif that his heart and intellect be peculiarly fit
in most matter of worldly import the archdeacon set down his father-in-law as little well than a fool
and perhaps he be right
but in some other matter equally important if they be rightly judge mr harding have he be so minded might with as much propriety have set down his son-in-law for a fool
few man however be constitute as be mr harding
he have that nice appreciation of the feeling of other which belong of right exclusively to woman
arm in arm they walk into the inner quadrangle of the building and there the five old man meet they
mr harding shake hand with they all and then mr quiverful do the same
with bunce mr harding shake hand twice and mr quiverful be about to repeat the ceremony but the old man give he no encouragement
i be very glad to know that at last you have a new warden say mr harding in a very cheery voice
we be very old for any change say one of they but we do suppose it be all for the good certainly--certainly it be for the good say mr harding
you will again have a clergyman of your own church under the same roof with you and a very excellent clergyman you will have
it be a great satisfaction to i to know that so good a man be come to take care of you and that it be no strange but a friend of my own who will allow i from time to time to come in and see you we be thankful to your reverence say another of they
i need not tell you my good friend say mr quiverful how extremely grateful i be to mr harding for his kindness to me i must say his uncalled for his unexpected kindness he be always very kind say a third
what i can do to fill the void which he leave here i will do
for your sake and my own i will do so and especially for his sake
but to you who have know he i can never be the same well-love friend and father that he have be no no sir say old bunce who hitherto have hold his peace no one can be that
not if the new bishop send a hangel to we from heaven
we donot doubt youwill do your good sir but youwill not be like the old master not to we old one fie bunce fie
how dare you talk in that way say mr harding but as he scold the old man he still hold he by his arm and press it with warm affection
there be no get any enthusiasm in the matter
how could five old man totter away to their final resting-place be enthusiastic on the reception of a stranger
what could mr quiverful be to they or they to mr quiverful
have mr harding indeed come back to they some last flicker of joyous light might have shine forth on their aged cheek but it be in vain to bid they rejoice because mr quiverful be about to move his fourteen child from puddingdale into the hospital house
in reality they do no doubt receive advantage spiritual as well as corporal but this they could neither anticipate nor acknowledge
it be a dull affair enough this introduction of mr quiverful but still it have its effect
the good which mr harding intend do not fall to the ground
all the barchester world include the five old bedesman treat mr quiverful with the more respect because mr harding have thus walk in arm in arm with he on his first entrance to his duty
and here in their new abode we will leave mr and mrs quiverful and their fourteen child
may they enjoy the good thing which providence have at length give to they
chapter liii conclusion the end of a novel like the end of a child s dinner-party must be make up of sweetmeat and sugar-plum
there be now nothing else to be tell but the gala doing of mr arabin s marriage nothing more to be describe than the wedding dress no further dialogue to be record than that which take place between the archdeacon who marry they and mr arabin and eleanor who be marry
wilt thou have this woman to thy wed wife and wilt thou have this man to thy wed husband to live together accord to god s ordinance mr arabin and eleanor each answer i will
we have no doubt that they will keep their promise the more especially as the signora neroni have leave barchester before the ceremony be perform
mrs bold have be somewhat more than two year a widow before she be married to her second husband and little johnnie be then able with due assistance to walk on his own leg into the drawing-room to receive the salutation of the assemble guest
mr harding give away the bride the archdeacon perform the service and the two miss grantlys who be join in their labour by other young lady of the neighbourhood perform the duty of bridesmaid with equal diligence and grace
mrs grantly superintend the breakfast and bouquet and mary bold distribute the card and cake
the archdeacon s three son have also come home for the occasion
the eld be great with learning be regard by all who know he as a certain future double first
the second however bear the palm on this occasion be resplendent in his new uniform
the third be just enter the university and be probably the proud of the three
but the most remarkable feature in the whole occasion be the excessive liberality of the archdeacon
he literally make present to everybody
as mr arabin have already move out of the parsonage of st ewold s that scheme of elongate the dining-room be of course abandon but he would have refurnish the whole deanery have he be allow
he send down a magnificent piano by erard give mr arabin a cob which any dean in the land might have be proud to bestride and make a special present to eleanor of a new pony chair that have gain a prize in the exhibition
nor do he even stay his hand here he buy a set of cameo for his wife and a sapphire bracelet for miss bold shower pearl and workbox on be daughter and to each of his son he present a cheque for 20 pound
on mr harding he bestow a magnificent violoncello with all the new-fashioned arrangement and expensive addition which on account of these novelty that gentleman could never use with satisfaction to his audience or pleasure to himself
those who know the archdeacon well perfectly understand the cause of his extravagance
twa thus that he sing his song of triumph over mr slope
this be his paean his hymn of thanksgiving his loud oration
he have gird himself with his sword and go forth to the war now he be return from the field laden with the spoil of the foe
the cob the cameo the violoncello and the pianoforte be all as it be trophy reft from the tent of his now conquer enemy
the arabins after their marriage go abroad for a couple of month accord the custom in such matter now duly establish and then commence their deanery life under good auspex
and nothing can be more pleasant than the present arrangement of ecclesiastical affair in barchester
the titular bishop never interfere and mrs proudie not often
her sphere be more extended more noble and more suited to her ambition than that of a cathedral city
as long as she can do what she please with the diocese she be willing to leave the dean and chapter to themselves
mr slope try his hand at subvert the old-establish custom of the close and from his failure she have learn experience
the burly chancellor and the meagre little prebendary be not tease by any application respect sabbath-day school the dean be leave to his own dominion and the intercourse between mrs proudie and mrs arabin be confine to a yearly dinner by each to the other
at these dinner dr grantly will not take a part but he never fail to ask for and receive a full account of all that mrs proudie do or say
his ecclesiastical authority have be greatly shorn since the palmy day in which he reign supreme as mayor of the palace to his father but nevertheless such authority as be now leave to he he can enjoy without interference
he can walk down high street of barchester without feel that those who see he be compare his claim with those of mr slope
the intercourse between plumstead and the deanery be of the most constant and familiar description
since eleanor have be marry to a clergyman and especially to a dignitary of the church mrs grantly have find many more point of sympathy with her sister and on a come occasion which be much look forward to by all party she intend to spend a month or two at the deanery
she never think of spend a month in barchester when little johnny bold be bear
the two sister do not quite agree on matter of church doctrine though their difference be of the most amicable description
mr arabin s church be two degree high than that of mrs grantly
this may seem strange to those who will remember that eleanor be once accuse of partiality to mr slope but it be no less the fact
she like her husband s silken vest she like his adherence to the rubric she specially like the eloquent philosophy of his sermon and she like the red letter in her own prayer-book
it must not be presume that she have a taste for candle or that she be at all astray about the real presence but she have an inkling that way
she send a handsome subscription towards certain very heavy legal expense which have lately be incur in bath her name of course not appear she assume a smile of gentle ridicule when the archbishop of canterbury be name and she have put up a memorial window in the cathedral
mrs grantly who belong to the high and dry church the high church as it be some fifty year since before tract be write and young clergyman take upon themselves the highly meritorious duty of clean church rather laugh at her sister
she shrug her shoulder and tell miss thorne that she suppose eleanor will have an oratory in the deanery before she have do
but she be not on that account a whit displease
a few high church vagary do not she think sit amiss on the shoulder of a young dean s wife
it show at any rate that her heart be in the subject and it show moreover that she be remove wide as the pole asunder from the cesspool of abomination in which it be once suspect that she would wallow and grovel
anathema maranatha
let anything be hold as bless so that that be well curse
welcome kneeling and bowing welcome matin and compline welcome bell book and candle so that mr slope s dirty surplice and ceremonial sabbath be hold in due execration
if it be essentially and absolutely necessary to choose between the two we be inclined to agree with mrs grantly that the bell book and candle be the less evil of the two
let it however be understand that no such necessity be admit in these page
dr arabin we suppose he must have become a doctor when he become a dean be more moderate and less outspoken on doctrinal point than his wife as indeed in his station it behove he to be
he be a studious thoughtful hard-work man
he live constantly at the deanery and preache nearly every sunday
his time be spend in sift and edit old ecclesiastical literature and in produce the same article new
at oxford he be generally regard as the most promising clerical ornament of the age
he and his wife live together in perfect mutual confidence
there be but one secret in her bosom which he have not share
he have never yet learn how mr slope have his ear box
the stanhopes soon find that mr slope s power need no long operate to keep they from the delight of their italian villa
before eleanor s marriage they have all migrate back to the shore of como
they have not be resettle long before the signora receive from mrs arabin a very pretty though very short epistle in which she be inform of the fate of the writer
this letter be answer by another bright charming and witty as the signora s always be and so end the friendship between eleanor and the stanhopes
one word of mr harding and we have do
he be still precentor of barchester and still pastor of the little church of st cuthbert s
in spite of what he have so often say himself he be not even yet an old man
he do such duty as fall to his lot well and conscientiously and be thankful that he have never be tempt to assume other for which he might be less fit
the author now leave he in the hand of his reader not as a hero not as a man to be admire and talk of not as a man who should be toast at public dinner and speak of with conventional absurdity as a perfect divine but as a good man without guile believe humbly in the religion which he strive to teach and guide by the precept which he have strive to learn the end
